desc:
The river is calm as it moves slowly eastwards away from the shadow of the
mountains in the west. Small water insects can be seen skimming the surface of
the water along with broken bits of reeds that float gently past. A sense of
peacefulness exudes from the small eddy pools formed by rocks in the current,
and the soothing sound of the waters gentle flow.
name: Tributary of the River

desc:
This vast plain has low vegetation that spreads out to the north. An extensive
vale with the Anduin river at its centre can be seen from here when the light is
right. The region to the north-west appears bushier than the valley while a
road, probably used by the Woodmen of long ago, lies to the south and west.
name: Vast Plain

desc:
Grass, about a foot tall, covers this land which is next to an old road running
along the south side of here. The land is low and continues a smooth descent
eastwards where the fertile ground is filled with many different types of
flowering plants. A wide valley, filled with various vegetation, opens to the
north where the plant life seems to flourish.
name: Grass beside the Road

desc:
The land here has effectively been invaded by multicoloured blossoms growing
among the opulent grass. An old forest road, that was used by the few Woodmen in
this region until the forest became exhausted long ago, can be seen south of
here. To the north is a small spring with very clear water. The flowers continue
to the east and west, though to the west the terrain has fewer flowers.
name: Flowery Grass

desc:
Fertile grass and large numbers of flowers of varying colours cover the ground
creating a view of health. The surrounding land forms an extensive valley that
gently slopes towards the flowing river Anduin in the far east. The flowers
continue westward and in the south an ancient road can still be seen.
name: Flowery Grass

desc:
This area is entirely covered with densely packed, thick-branched bushes. The
leaves of the bushes look very large with vigorous blossoms when the season is
right. A wide valley opens to the north and is covered with bushes, while the
land in the west appears grassy with an abundance of flowers. An old abandoned
road runs beside this area to the south.
name: Plump Shrubbery

desc:
The road makes a gentle turn here, continuing both south and west. Plump
shrubbery intermixed with several thick clumps of bushes lies on the north side
of the road. Short grass and rough spots make it appear as if the road has seen
little usage for many years.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
These low, bushy fields are covered with grass and bushes of varying height. The
bushes are low, but their branches spread out quite a bit. Thick clusters have
been formed by many different species of brush - even an occasional tree can be
spotted in a cluster. The bushes seem to stretch long tangles of branches
towards the road, reaching for ground as yet uncovered by the thick brambles. To
the south and west the road turns.
name: Fields beside the Road

desc:
These low, rolling fields stretch out unbroken by hill or woods under the skies
above. The fields are mainly covered by grass, but many bushes and thickets of
bramble grow here, almost creating a maze on the plain. In these thickets the
sounds of small animals running around can be heard, as well as birds calling
out to each other from their hiding spots in the foliage.
name: Rolling Fields

desc:
This section of the road remains almost intact, with few bushes and other plants
on it. The lack of plants is possibly a result of the quarry that lies to the
east of here - the barren old rockface is not very fertile and no plant spores
are sent onto this road from there. The road leads west to the Misty Mountains
in the distance, and east through fields on its way to the Anduin River.
name: Bend around a Quarry

desc:
This is what seems to be an old abandoned quarry. The miners that once dug and
broke stone here are long gone, but this place is like an old wound, inflicted
by man and slow to heal. Some small, lonely flowers grow in pockets of earth
among the otherwise barren stone. In all directions, except to the south, there
stand fifteen-feet-high stonewalls that prevent travel by mounts but a skilled
climber could ascend the western wall.
name: An Old Quarry

desc:
North of the road lies a quarry, on whose west and south sides the road goes
around. The road leads west to the mountains, and east to the ford in the Anduin
River. It appears to be more like a wide, beaten earth path here with no stones
in it. Perhaps the last visitors to the quarry took the last stones from the
road instead of quarrying their own. The road appears to have seen little
traffic for many years, and no care has been given to it for some time before
that.
name: Road South of a Quarry

desc:
These rocky plains are the western shore of the Anduin River. There, in the
river, old ruins can be spotted, standing out strangely above the waterline. To
the south lies an old ford, which allows passage across to the eastern lands of
the Vale of Anduin.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The clear water here reveals the destruction and waste that litters the
riverbed. Ruins toward the east beside the river indicate the source of the
large chunks of abandoned wood and metal, apparently thrown in by hand.
Nevertheless, the river continues to flow south past all these obstacles to
where it becomes very shallow. Some vegetation grows along the water's edge.
name: Ruins in the Anduin River

desc:
Ancient ruins of a long forgotten building stand here, with moss-covered stone
blocks and broken logs standing from the ground. The surrounding grasslands are
slowly reclaiming these ruins. To the south the old forest road can be seen and
to the west the mighty Anduin river can be heard.
name: Ruins of the Past

desc:
Debris from the old ruins to the north has fallen to cover part of the northern
edge of the old forest road. Small elm and oak saplings grow along both sides of
the road. To the west, a ford over the Anduin River is visible.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The field is covered with lush, green grass that sways when the wind blows.
Scattered about the grass are yellow and blue flowers. A pile of stones lies in
the field, the stones grey with lichen. To the east a clump of trees can be seen
and a bit to the west there appears to be a ford through the river.
name: Fields by the Road

desc:
Old elm trees and ancient oaks grow amongst each other here blocking much of the
wind. The air is damp with moisture, the ground covered with dead, decaying logs
and moss-covered rocks. Vines cling to the trees and climb up their trunks to
reach for the light above the canopy. An animal track seems to lead to the old
forest road that can be sighted through the trees to the south.
name: A Dense Clump of Trees

desc:
West of this road is a dark clump of trees around which the road bends.
Eastwards the road continues straight for some distance. Saplings from the elms
and oaks west of here grow along the edge of the road. To the southeast a small
hill stands.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
This grass-covered ridge declines towards the road in the south. A small trail
runs along the top of the ridge to where it connects with the road. Some stones
lie about the sloping sides and thorny bushes grow amongst them. To the north
this ridge stands between two fields, and to the west there is an open vale. The
road south of here has a bend in it.
name: Southern Slope of a Ridge

desc:
Foot-high yellow grass grows here, with the blades shaped like long, thin
arrowheads. West of here there is a grassy ridge, while to the south an old road
can be seen. A dark forest stands tall in the far east.
name: Fields by the Road

desc:
A small village can be seen halting the eastward journey of the Old Forest Road
a few miles from here. The road takes a wide turn to the south before running up
to the village walls. In the north, a collection of hills and levees form a
semicircle, blocking in water-soaked plains sitting at a lower elevation.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Off in the east, the tops of elm trees can be seen just above a village. The
road leads up to the village but does not appear to continue past it.
Immediately to the north, the ground slopes downwards into a low basin in the
Anduin Vales. From this elevated vantage, travellers could make out the muddy
lay of the lands in the north.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The road continues to the east and west for a few miles before bending away.
This road lies in a state of disrepair as it is well-used here but ill cared
for. To the south, weeds creep up to the road, crossing it and filling in
potholes. In the north, the lands falls down into a large depression in the
plains.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The Old Forest Road stretches in an east-west direction here. To the east is a
distant and huge, dark forest. Far to the west mist-covered mountains rise to
the sky. The road does not appear to be used very often, if at all. Grass and
tiny bushes grow in old ruts on the roadbed.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The riverbed here is high enough to make it possible for people and creatures to
cross. The water runs deep and swift in spots, with stones protruding from the
surface in places. Anyone losing their footing might be dashed against some of
those boulders. Both upriver and downriver the river is much deeper. The grass
from the surrounding fields grows right to the water's edge, with some of it
dangling in the water.
name: Old Ford

desc:
The road broadens here into a large open area resembling a trampled square of
earth more than a road. Small patches of grass grow in small clusters, along
with an odd flower or two. To the east lies the Old Ford, one of the few places
one can safely cross the Anduin River. To the west, a long unused road runs
through the plains.
name: West of the Old Ford

desc:
From here the old road heads straight east to the ford across the Anduin River
and north towards an abandoned quarry. The road seems to be slightly better
repaired than farther to the northwest - why, one can only guess. To the west,
dense brush covers the plain, and some vines have stretched out towards the
road, longing for light and space to expand into. To the south lies an old
farmstead.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The main plants here are dense tangles of knee-high bushes with arrowhead-like
leaves. The only undergrowth that lives beneath the bushes are thick, thorny
weeds that cling to the ground. To the west stands the border of a forest.
Eastwards a road makes its way through the uneven terrain.
name: Dense Bushes

desc:
The birch forest that flourishes west of here is much thinner in this area. The
birch trees grow farther apart here, making it easier to travel through this
part of the forest. High grass and low bushes also grow here, enjoying the light
that filters down through the treetops during the day.
name: Birchwood Border

desc:
The luxuriant birch forest flourishes without restraint in this dank and vital
terrain thereby inhibiting the growth of smaller plants. The trees grow very
densely packed, making travel through this forest difficult. The forest becomes
more mixed to the south but continues to be dominated by birches in the west.
name: Luscious Birchwood

desc:
Large bushes with long green leaves cover this area along the road. A few tiny
mountain ashes have managed to sprout here and struggle for nearly non-existent
living space. The ground is teeming with roots of different sizes making travel
unpleasant. The land declines gently northward.
name: Scrubland

desc:
Great downy birches grow tall in this luxuriant terrain giving excellent
protection and food for the animals that inhabit the area. The ground is very
soft and there is a thick undergrowth with various small, decaying plants. This
entirely birch forest continues to the east but becomes less purely birch in the
south.
name: Birch Forest

desc:
The road turns once again, snaking its way through the overgrown plains. The
road was once broad and well looked-after, but only a wide cart-track remains.
The edges and ditches of the road have already been taken over by creeping
bushes. The road bends to the north and east, while to the south and west wild
bushes cover the plain.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
Sturdy bushes with wide, green leaves grow quite densely here. Both to the north
and to the east of here is an old road. The bushes here spread out into the
lands to the west. To the south appears to be the edge of a forest.
name: Dense Bushes

desc:
The hard packed soil, that was at one time a road, has lost its fight with
nature. Several varieties of flowers, bushes and grass have loosened the soil
enough so that they can flourish here. East and west the road runs from the ford
in the mighty Anduin river to the mountains west of here.
name: The Old Forest Road

desc:
Large bushes and a few mountain ashes grow along the south side of the road
while along the western side grows a short grass, together with some flowers and
a few small bushes. The road appears quite smooth and a couple of wagon tracks
can be seen on the grass-covered surface.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The land here is completely covered with short grass, many types of flowers, and
a few small bushes scattered about. An old forest road passes by the north and
east of here. The eastern road provides a well marked path to the river Anduin.
The grassy plain continues to the west while the lands in the south are home to
larger plants.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
The road is bordered by luxuriant vegetation consisting of various kinds of
flowers and bushes that seem to flourish without restraint. The road descends to
the east where the Anduin vale opens wide, with its river near the centre.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Fertile vegetation consisting of different types of flowers and several bushes
border this ancient road which descends eastwards to where an extensive valley
opens. The road appears quite unused and much of it has become covered by short
grass.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Bountiful grass and a few small bushes grow across this plain just south of the
road. Tracks of animals are easily visible when some are passing through because
of the soft soil. The land rises gently to the west where a bushy embankment
rises. A small clearing opens out in the south.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
A small clearing covered only with short grass is here. The soft soil can easily
show many tracks from various animals that sometimes pass this way. The lands to
the north and west are nondescript but towards the east and south the terrain
turns rugged and a number of small bushes appear.
name: A Clearing

desc:
The land is overlaid by a large number of small bushes, many of which have had
their leaves consumed by maggots. Meanwhile, a few birds fly overhead looking
for small insects and maggots to feast upon. The bushy ground continues
southward where the bushes grow more irregularly. A forest of green birches
covers the land east of here.
name: Small Bushes

desc:
Thickets of holly bushes are scattered about, leaving fairly spacious routes
between them. Spiky hawthorns unfold their nasty twigs and one must be careful
in order to avoid being scratched. The passages between the bushes open into a
small clearing to the west.
name: Sparse Thicket

desc:
This grassy land contains an even mix of hay and bushes with a few molehills
scattered about. The surrounding lands look the same except to the east where
the bushes grow more densely.
name: Grassland

desc:
The grassy land gently slopes down towards the west where a muddy-looking trail
leads towards a village in the distant west. Hummocks of trefoil can be seen
growing in several places forming a quilt-like pattern before you. The grassy
land continues in all directions but the west.
name: Grassland

desc:
Large bushes, with thick leaves, grow on this sharp bank which borders a road to
the north. Some of the bushes are missing leaves, having been consumed by
insects and herbivores which occupy the luxuriant terrain. The ground descends
gently southward where it is covered by grass and a few bushes. To the west
there is a muddy trail which joins the road in the north-west.
name: Bushy Bank

desc:
The ground here is covered with numerous bushes of various sizes and types. All
of the bushes look healthy though some of the branches have been gnawed by
animals feeding in this area. The bushy embankment continues westward and runs
parallel to the road just north of here while the lands to the east and south
are covered with grass.
name: Bushy Bank

desc:
Short parched grass grows on this otherwise uninhabited plain. The once soft
ground has become rather dried and broken due to long periods of sunshine and
infrequent rain. The land rises gently northward where a bushy embankment
stands. Grasslands continue to the south and west while a small clearing among
the bushes is visible to the east.
name: Loamy Grassland

desc:
A muddy trail bypasses this grassy field to the west. Here, the ground is very
rough and supports some divergent flowers and a few insignificant bushes. The
ground gently rises northward where it forms an embankment covered with bushes.
The grass continues to the east and south.
name: Grassland

desc:
Towards the east the road descends until it joins the mouth of a very large
valley, while to the west the road continues until it branches north and south.
On the south side of the road there is an embankment that is covered by large
bushes. On the north side of the road lies a vast plain with short grass growing
on it.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
An embankment, containing various types of bushes, lies to the south side of
this infrequently used road. The road descends towards the east. North of the
road is a grassy area. The road itself looks to be very old and in poor shape
after having been deeply rutted by the passage of wagons many years ago.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
This is the border of a tremendous vale which opens to the east. The ground here
is covered with short grass and some bushes which are spread out across the
grass. The edge of the valley continues to the north while to the south there is
a vast plain covered by hay. A clearing lies cloaked in haze westward of here.
name: The Valley Edge

desc:
The plain is covered with short grass and a few small bushes. The ground
declines smoothly eastward where it joins a large valley filled with wildly
growing plants. Westward a mysterious mist rises from a large, strange looking
hollow that is covered with thick bushes.
name: Vast Plain

desc:
The vegetation in this bountiful valley is very luxuriant and bears a rich
growth of plants during the warm months of late spring and early summer. Land
such as this forms the perfect environment for plant eaters and the animals that
feed on them. The tracks of several animals are visible as well as a few small
bones.
name: Blooming Vale

desc:
The ground slopes gently around this tranquil spring where the crystal-clear
water slowly trickles out of the ground. The water, no more than two feet deep,
has collected around the spring forming a small pond. The bottom of the pond is
covered with fine sand and a few snails. The lands surrounding the spring are
flourishing with both plant and animal life and the singing of birds can be
heard for many miles.
name: The Limpid Spring

desc:
Rampantly growing weeds and rotting crops have taken over this dormant land
which was once cultivated but nowadays is untended. What became of the farmer
that once farmed this land is unknown. A flimsy-looking hut stands along the
northern edge of the field and apparently was the home of the farmer.
name: Abandoned Field

desc:
Various sized bushes, most of them looking quite luxuriant, fill the valley. The
soil is smooth and very soft, it readily absorbs the tracks of animals, both
large and small, on its surface. An untended farm field is located westward next
to a small hut. North of here the valley becomes lifeless.
name: Bushy Valley

desc:
Bushes of various sizes cling to the steep slope that rises upwards to the east.
Nearby to the west is a valley filled with bushes, beyond it are grasslands over
which the Misty Mountains loom.
name: Bushy Slope

desc:
In this small valley the grass that dominates the lands to the east is broken up
by large bushes growing in densely packed clumps. The clumps of bushes grow more
numerous to the west and to the north a bush covered slope can be seen.
name: Small Valley

desc:
A sharp incline rises up from the flat surface of the southern fields. East of
here the slant is somewhat shallower, while to the north it appears more
treacherous and steep. A few small plants sprout out of the incline among the
generally uninterrupted blades of grass.
name: Corner of a Slope

desc:
Lush green grass grows lightly on this small slope. The slightest breeze makes
the long grass sway in waves along the ground. A few sparse elms grow here,
multiplying towards the north and northwest. The slant of the slope is equal in
the easterly and westerly directions.
name: Light, Grassy Slope

desc:
Covering most of this small hill are bushes of various types. Solid rock shows
through spots on the mainly grass-covered southern slope of this hill. To the
south lies the edge of a drop into an old quarry.
name: Small Hill

desc:
The earth here is gravelly, with matted-down grass growing on it. Clusters of
foot-high bushes dot the surrounding area. To the east there is a steep
embankment leading down to the river Anduin and to the north and west the
terrain rises.
name: Bushes by the Anduin

desc:
Rocks ranging in size from pebbles to fist-sized stones are scattered across the
ground. The land declines into the Anduin River east of here where the stones
continue and have been smoothed by the constant flow over them. To the west
there is an old quarry, while south and north of here there are no distinctive
landmarks.
name: Pebbled Riverbank

desc:
The water of the Anduin River is quite clear and the sandy bottom is visible
although it is far below. It appears that the river begins to lose some of its
depth as it flows south and deepens towards the north. There is short grass and
small plants thriving on the river's edge but only trees grow on the eastern
bank.
name: Anduin River

desc:
Elm trees of various sizes grow away from the bank of the river. Closer by the
trees give way to bushes of mixed varieties. The grass is high and deep- green
in colour. Flowers of various colours grow around the base of the elm trees and
in patches between them. South of here some ancient ruins can be seen.
name: Elms by a Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The remains of what appears to be a razed stone shed are lying about in the
grass here. Grass and bushes grow amongst the old stones, with some moss growing
on the stones. To the west, some trees can be seen and to the east there is a
vale inside the fields.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
Small stone walls split the grassland here. The walls stand at several places in
the field and saxifrages grow among the stones. Old hurdle poles covered with
wild roses were placed in this grassland as well. To the west is an old ruined
building.
name: Fields by the Ruins

desc:
This open vale spreads out north and west. To the east the land climbs up a
grassy ridge, and a dark clump of trees can be seen to the south. Some wild
roses grow in this vale.
name: A Vale in the Fields

desc:
A lonely, large oak tree is standing here in this vale, surrounded by grass and
purple flowers. To the north and south the vale continues. There appears to be a
ridge rising from the fields to the east.
name: A Vale in the Fields

desc:
The ridge stretches in a north-south direction and is covered by grass and rocks
of different shapes and sizes. A few scattered elms grow on top of this ridge.
To the south, the ridge appears to come to a gradual end, but it continues to
the north. West of the ridge a vale sits in the middle of the fields. A trail
runs along the length of the ridge.
name: A Ridge in the Fields

desc:
Flowing in from the east and curving back towards the south, the water here
flows swiftly and at times overflows the riverbanks. Low shrubs and various
greenery spreads in great masses along the sides of the river. Distant, but
looming, in the west are the Misty Mountains.
name: Anduin River Bend

desc:
Throughout this small area bordering the Anduin River there is a thin, light
grass growing; however, near the edges of the river, it sprouts somewhat
thicker. Shorter plants and some small trees hang over the river. To the
northwest some grassy fields rise.
name: Light Grass by the Anduin

desc:
The current of the Anduin runs quickly here; the water is so deep and murky that
the bottom is not visible. Branches, leaves, and roots hang limply from the
trees and bushes into the water. Some longer, weed-like growths wave back and
forth in the water, played with by the current.
name: Anduin River

desc:
A river from the west of here flows into the Anduin at this point, stirring up
mud from the riverbed and causing the water to foam from the competing currents.
The land to the north is constantly flooded by water from the rivers around it.
There is a heavy concentration of rocks and boulders in the water here.
name: A Tributary Meets the Anduin River

desc:
Out of the muddy ground here grows thick, dark-green grass. Short vegetation
grows sparsely here, becoming denser towards the west, south, and east, where
the sound of rushing water comes from. Towards the north, the grass begins to
lengthen and it covers the ground completely.
name: Sodden Turf

desc:
This portion of the river flows rapidly over its uneven bed of pebbles and
larger rocks. As the water follows along its course here, it bends toward the
south. Some of the water overflows the eastern bank to flood onto the grass east
of here.
name: Bend in the Small River

desc:
The Anduin River flows calmly along, its current slow but steady on its way to
the distant south. Grassy fields stretch from the river's edge outwards on both
its east and west banks. The riverbed is littered with tiny pebbles and sand,
colouring it beige and helping to camouflage its inhabitants.
name: Anduin River

desc:
The river flows into here from the east and then turns southward. Long grass
from the western side dips over the edge of the bank and hangs into the water
where the current plays with it. The river depth appears constant along this
stretch, but the pace slows as it curves southwards.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The riverbank here is mostly marshy, with expanses of lichens and moss lying
scattered around. Many pebbles have been washed ashore by the rapid pace of the
rushing waters as the Anduin River continues its journey, heading into the
southern plains. A vast array of plains and fields lie settled over the western
landscape and an endless quantity of grassland covers the land to the
north-west.
name: Marshy Riverbank along the Anduin River

desc:
The river slowly finds its way between the sloping grounds both to the east and
west, and can be seen bending to the right further ahead. Lush plains at the
east bank seem to continue for a few miles before rising slopes hide the
grassland beyond them from your view. Several willows lining the other shore are
bent low over the flow.
name: The River Anduin

desc:
As the unstoppable flow of the Great River completes one more of its slow,
countless bends, it takes up once again its main course towards the distant
south, in its endless way through the green grasslands of the Anduin Vale. High
and immutable tower the Misty Mountains to the west, like a row of heavy stone
sentinels; but many brooks rise also in their snowy peaks, tributary of the
greatest of Middle-earth's rivers.
name: Bend in the River Anduin

desc:
Plains start to descend steeply towards the river, with the actual riverbank
standing several feet high above the steadily flowing waters, making their way
between some rather vicious-looking sharp rocks and boulders near the bank.
Overlooking the river, sloping plains go on as far as the eye can see, and are
framed by the snow-covered tops of the Misty Mountains beyond the western
skyline.
name: Riverside Plain

desc:
The plains start rising steeply, away from the Anduin Valley to the west. From
here, you can see the river cutting through the landscape and grasslands,
disappearing eventually between the hills of the southern plains. To the north
you spot a lone rock standing tall above the flow of the Anduin a few miles
away. Over the slopes and valleys to the west, the tops of the Misty Mountains
frame the view.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
Grass grows among the scattered pebbles that dot the ground. A small but
distinct animal trail cuts through the green grass here. It avoids the larger
rocks which gives the trail a serpentine look. Far to the north a lone rock can
be seen and rising to the southwest there appears to be a mound of stones.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
The bushes here somewhat break the monotony of the grass that covers this land.
The bushes are of various sizes, some with branches as thick as a hobbit's arm.
There appear to be quite a few rabbits here considering all the piles of sand
that rise from the ground among the bushes. To the west is a mound of stones.
name: Bushy Fields

desc:
The plains here are completely overgrown with dense brush of all kinds, some
short, some tall, some sporting colourful flowers between sharp thorns. Nature
is thus - amidst the ugly one may find beauty. Among the top branches small
birds have made their nests and can sometimes be seen trying to hide, looking
for food or even singing now and then. To the south is a hillock amongst the
fields.
name: Dense Brush

desc:
Among the various bushes on the sides of this hill lie scattered moss-covered
boulders. An animal track circumvents the rocks with the western end seeming to
lead to a tarn that can be sighted through the bushes there.
name: A Hillock in the Fields

desc:
The tall grasses that grow here are of many varieties, some with thin and
seemingly brittle blades, some more robust with deep, rich colour. To the south
the fields seem to have been cultivated by man due to the rich earth there. To
the east, the land dips into a slight depression.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Here, on the eastern slope of a ridge, the people of the village have planted a
field of turnips. The land appears to be quite well tended from the lack of
weeds among the turnip crop. West of here stands a wooden palisade.
name: Turnip Field

desc:
Clover grows in huge patches here with lush, green grass covering the rest of
the pasture. The pasture is a paradise for the cattle the men of the village are
breeding. Northwest of here, in the plains, lies the village that owns this
pasture. To the west is a grassy ridge amidst the fields.
name: Pastureland by a Ridge

desc:
This semi-cultivated field lies directly north of the village palisade. All
sorts of bushes and grasses and plants grow in this field. Deciding which have
been planted by man and are edible, and which are wild is difficult at best, but
clearly the inhabitants of this land would know.
name: North of a Village

desc:
Embraced by green grass and small bushes a small tarn lies in the middle of this
widespread field. The water of the tarn is brown but by the amount of animal
tracks that can be seen in the mud bordering it this is a very well-used
waterhole.
name: A Small Tarn

desc:
On these bushy plains large rocks and boulders lie strewn about as if they had
been thrown by giants. A more reasonable explanation would be that they fell or
slid from the mound of stones that can be seen to the north, as the mound does
not appear to have been tended for quite some time. Between the stones grows
grass with many beautiful flowers peeping out from it, adding a colour to the
otherwise green and grey landscape. To the far south one can spot a palisade,
while to the west, in the distance, the Anduin River lies.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
Stones and boulders of different shapes and sizes have been piled into a large
stone mound here. The stones, that are not covered with greyish moss, appear to
be well weathered and give hints as to how ancient this mound is and how long
ago it was created.
name: A Mound of Stones

desc:
The twisting Anduin River that rushes southwards to the west of here has flooded
these grasslands, making the ground muddy with patches of water. Tracks can
easily be seen in the mud when something passes through. South of here, stones
of varying sizes have been piled into a high mound.
name: Flooded Plains

desc:
The plains are descending to the river, which is bending directly west of here
and continuing towards the horizon through the southern plains as far as an eye
can see. Eastwards, the ground is becoming more and more hilly as it rises
slowly. Far beyond those hillocks, the outskirts of Mirkwood must lie.
name: Lush Plains by the River

desc:
The river makes a gracious curve westwards, only to bend south again within a
couple of miles further down the flow, and continue its endless journey through
the borders of Rohan all the way to the salty waters of the Great Sea. On both
banks, sloping grasslands roll on seemingly forever, with the snowy peaks of the
Misty Mountains barely more than a thin white stripe at the western horizon.
name: The River Anduin

desc:
The Anduin River flows from the north and then curves toward the west. Dark-
green, long grass grows from the edges of the south and east sides of the river
into the distance. The water is clear as crystal, and the river bottom is far
below the surface.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The ground here is very sandy where not covered by grass or the large number of
rocks and boulders here. Long, dark-green grass begins at the edge of the river,
that flows west of here, and continues to the mound that sits to the east.
name: Grasslands by the River

desc:
The sandy shore gently declines into the Anduin river that flows to the west of
here. Some lonely holly bushes are growing in the sand along with the occasional
patch of long, dark grass.
name: Sandy Shore

desc:
The soil is mainly of a sandy constitution, and thus only certain varieties of
grass grow here - long, pale greyish-blue stalks that rattle stiffly when the
wind blows. Among these grasses live small animals that scurry around looking
for food and materials to build their nests and hollows. To the west the great
Anduin river swiftly and strongly flows on its voyage to the sea.
name: Sandy Grassland

desc:
The grass does not dominate the landscape here like in many of the areas nearby.
Blackberry bushes grow everywhere here, to the pleasure of the birds that come
here for them. A small trail runs among the bushes giving a hint that not only
birds travel to here for food, but that people from the village also come here
for the berries.
name: Among the Blackberry Bushes

desc:
This grass-covered section of the vale extends into the south where a large
lonely tree can be seen above the tall grass. A large wooden palisade made of
long logs makes it impossible to go farther east here.
name: West of a Village

desc:
A small boat ramp leads down into the mighty Anduin River flowing west of here.
There are three small sheds built around a campfire site. The ground is
trampled, giving a hint that this area is used frequently by some group of
people in the Vale. A few elm trees grow along the bank of the river.
name: Old Fishing Camp

desc:
The Anduin River flows smoothly from the north and abruptly bends to the west.
Along the eastern edge of the river a few elm trees grow amongst tall and wavy
grass. The river bottom is sandy and littered with small stones and pebbles, but
their shapes and sizes are somewhat distorted by the water's depth.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
Oak trees of various sizes are growing on this side of the Anduin River. The
branches of the bigger oaks that grow close to the shore hang over the river
like long fingers. The ground is covered with old, dead leaves from the trees.
name: Oaks Bordering the Anduin River

desc:
The small oak saplings growing here are likely seedlings of the older oak trees
that can be seen to the south. North and west of here the mighty Anduin river
flows by. To the east there are no trees, only grasses.
name: Fields by a Bend in the River

desc:
The water here flows smoothly as it comes from the north and bends towards the
west. Long grass grows in patches to the east of the river, and thrives to the
south. The slightly rocky and sandy river bottom does not appear to be far away,
however the water is far too deep for one to stand in.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
The ground here is rich with moist soil caused by the rushing water of the
Anduin River, which lies to the east. Much of the land here has survived, rich
with nutrients but poor in cultivation, with the help of the nearby water
source. Pebbles have been washed up ashore with the rapid pace of the rushing
waters as the Anduin River continues its journey heading in the south and seems
endless from this view point.
name: Riverbank along the Anduin River

desc:
Distinct from the surrounding landscape to the north-west, you can notice the
transition of widely dense grasslands to slowly disbursed plains of shrubs and
patches of grass. This lively land can be seen to come to an end as you peer off
into the easternmost direction, spotting the River Anduin. The great river
continues to flow to the south in a rapid pace extending to the uttermost
distance as far as the eye can see.
name: Disperse Plains

desc:
The Anduin River has had a great impact on the surrounding land. The soil has
become very marshy with its many shrubs, moss and lichens that grow out from the
ruptured ground. The Great River extends in the far distance to the south and
continues to flow as far as the eye can see. Large lily pads near the water's
shore indicate a form of wildlife such as frogs or toads. Other forms of life
can be observed such as the swarms of insects that can be seen on the edge near
the riverbank of the river.
name: Marshy Riverbank

desc:
Small, misshapen pine trees are scattered about the quagmire. Among grass grows
an occasional clump of bulrushes. There is a constant buzz from the insects that
swarm here when conditions are right. To the east, the mighty Anduin River flows
on its way south.
name: Quagmire by the Anduin River

desc:
Stunted pine trees grow in this quagmire, which marks the edge of the river to
the west. Along the water a compact wall of bullrushes sways with even the
slightest of winds. On the ground some wood-grouse droppings can be seen among
the cloud-berry bushes that stand above the grass.
name: Quagmire by the River

desc:
The river flowing along here from the west eventually reaches a bend and diverts
toward the south. The bottom of the river is muddy, which provides nutrients for
underwater plants, but clouds the water.
name: Bend in the Muddy River

desc:
The river here deepens as it flows in from the north and slowly bends toward the
east. The edges of the river are green with vegetation that lives off the river
and the nutrients it carries. Moving eastwards the river begins to flow faster
as it travels over a riverbed made of stones and rock.
name: Deepening River

desc:
Mixed with the thick green grass are many small white and pink clover flowers.
The clover grows in dense clumps throughout the field giving the land a spotted
appearance. In the eastern part of the field, there are some jagged rocks that
stick up from the ground like sharp teeth. Both to north and east the sound of
running water can be heard and to the south a gentle slope can be seen.
name: Clover Fields

desc:
The river here runs a twisted route from the north into here, then flowing out
to the east on its way to the Anduin River. The water churns and foams as a
result of numerous mid-size rocks standing in the way of the rushing current of
the river.
name: Rushing River

desc:
Short grass grows here with a border of riverside plants along the banks of the
Anduin River to the east. Little life appears in this sparsely covered area near
the river. This region continues in much the same way towards the south. The
field begins to incline upwards in the west.
name: Grass near the River

desc:
The ground here continually rises towards the north, and declines southwards.
The grass grows uniformly, hiding from view many small dips and ditches that
litter the slope. Eastward the incline is gentler, while the decline to the west
is the same as here.
name: Grassy Incline

desc:
This grassy slope is only slightly elevated from the grasslands to the east and
north. Small flowers sprout in little patches throughout the soft, ankle-deep
grass. In the westerly direction distant trees interrupt the normal horizon
which is dominated in the other directions by low growing plants.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
Ancient willow trees, their trunks withered by age, grow here in a straight
north-south line. They were planted like this a long time ago to act as a
windbreak that would prevent fertile soil from being blown away by the wind.
Amongst the green grasses are golden coloured wild oats which add a lush look to
the field.
name: Avenue of Willows

desc:
Several towering elms grow widely spaced on this easy slope. However, aside from
the immediate area around here, there are no trees to be seen in the distance.
The entire slope is uniformly covered with thick grass, which continues in all
directions.
name: Grassy Slope

desc:
This slope, which is lightly covered by bushes, declines steeply to the west,
and ascends to the east. Some sparse elm trees grow straight into the sky in
spite of the steep slant to this hill. Many of the elms' huge, thick roots can
be seen sticking from the soil. The slope is just as steep southwards as it is
here, but to the slope to the north appears to be gentler.
name: Steep Incline

desc:
The land here appears to be quite dry and therefore it is hard for the larger
plants to thrive. Grass and a few thorn bushes manage to grow here. In the dry
grass an animal trail can been seen passing through to the north and south.
name: Dry Field

desc:
This flat area is covered with two varieties of grasses. In the southern part of
the field the grass is short and stumpy and has a yellowish colour, whereas
along the northern edge the grass is green and tall. To the south the land rises
up away from the sound of running water that comes from the north.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
The flow of water over the underwater plants causes them to wave around and stir
up the muddy bottom of this river, removing any transparency it might have had.
Aside from the plant-life within the river itself, only plants on the
surrounding edges drink from the water to support themselves. Beyond that margin
of plants, larger plants cannot survive.
name: Muddy River

desc:
Not much life is able to thrive here within the confined area dictated by the
small stream and the dry path which you walk along. There are, however, a few
trees and other forms of vegetation that can be seen within close distance. A
small stream with muddy and shallow shores can be made out to the south and
running waters can be observed heading in the eastern direction.
name: Barren Plains along a Shallow Stream

desc:
The ground beneath you is somewhat hard and lifeless in this area. To the east
are more plains like the one around you now and to the south is a small river,
which from this distance looks rather brown and muddy. To the west is an old
crop field which would have supported some bountiful crops but due to the lack
of upkeep, it has just withered away like most of the land to the west.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The ground is unable to provide nutrients to any plant or grass life with the
lack of water around here. There are more plains in all directions, but the
vegetation seems just a bit more dense to the west. Looking further to the south
is a flowing stream of muddy waters.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
Looking closer at the land around you there is not much change in the growing
vegetation and plant life. The only sense of direction that can be of help is
the muddy stream to the south of you. Looking far to the east, you can see a
large body of water flowing rapidly. Everything else around you seems very
motionless and inactive depicting the impression of a huge wasteland.
name: Barren Plains along a Muddy Stream

desc:
The river here is opaque from the stirring of mud caused by the movement of
underwater plants within it. Dark green and brown protrusions of branches and
leaves stick out of the water, causing ripples in the slowly flowing water.
name: Shallow and Muddy River

desc:
Not much remains of this old settlement; it appears to have been burnt to the
ground a long time ago. The only thing still standing is an old battered chimney
covered with ramblers and ivy. To the north, and the east, the sound of flowing
water can be heard.
name: Old Settlement

desc:
Walking along these barren plains makes it easy to be confused as to whether you
are going around in circles. The large expanse of green to the north gives off
the impression that there is much grassland in that direction. Observing the
route of the muddy stream it seems to head off into the southern direction. The
sound of raging waters can be heard more clearly now, just as if you can almost
distinguish you are getting closer to the source of the river.
name: Barren Plains near a Muddy Stream

desc:
The earth is dead here with its lack of fields of flowers or any other sort of
plant life. These plains extend in all directions except for the slight growth
of grasslands to the northern direction. Looking closer to the south you can see
a muddy river which stretches out in the eastern direction. A great river can
also be seen from this point, heading into the east will only bring you closer.
name: Deserted Plains

desc:
As the plains come to an end in this area, out come vast fields of grassland
that encompass the surrounding land to the north and east. Short, shallow-rooted
grasses, often growing in bunches covering large areas, can be seen nearby. To
the south lie plains which form a desolate and lifeless landscape, giving off
the impression of uninhabited lands.
name: Feeble Plains

desc:
The landscape around here is very desolate and composed of dry land seemingly
devoid of life. To the east of you lies a great river of enormous power which
flows in a north and south direction. Further to the south of here is a muddy
river which is rich in brown colours due to its vast amount of muddy water. Some
fields of green can be seen to the north.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
No flowers or bushes grow in this land that is battered by winds that tend to
blow from the west. In the shelter of some sharp rocks some patches of short,
yellowish grass struggle to make this land appear a bit more fertile.
name: Windswept Field

desc:
The ground here is nearly flat and very monotonous. Short, stumpy grass can be
seen in all directions. There does not seem to be any memorable landmarks
visible from here, but the sound of running water can be heard from the north.
name: Flat Grassland

desc:
Water from the north flows through here and then towards the east. The water is
deep enough to immerse many of the taller plants which grow in the water. Beyond
the mixed types of plants growing along the edge of the river is a grassy field.
name: Bend in the Small River

desc:
A steep slope to the east blocks the flow of the stream and forces it to flow
onward in the southern direction. The stream continues through the plains and
lowlands towards the Anduin Vale. Lush vegetation and a couple of trees make
this place look less barren than the surrounding plains.
name: A Small Waterfall

desc:
There are strong indications in this portion of land that once, long ago,
someone or something took great care to cultivate it. All that remains are a few
patches of wheat and corn which have survived through the ages on their own
through the harsh seasons that have passed by, year after year. Towards the west
an extension of this old field can be seen, along with an object which portrays
the shape of a tombstone. The sound of rushing waters can be heard to the south.
name: Old Crop Field

desc:
The people who once tended this backyard are long gone. A half tilled patch of
land leads one to believe that the caretakers departed suddenly. The plants are
withered and dying. The land has dried up from a lack of watering and anything
that might have once flourished is now in a state of decay. At the centre of the
yard there is a small grave plot covered by a mound of loose soil with a large
tombstone at the head.
name: Old Backyard

desc:
This small river is slowly but steadily working its way to the east. A few large
stones line the bottom of the river along the northern edge, perhaps thrown into
the water from the barren farmland there. The plain to the south seems to have a
richer soil than the land to the north and its plants are thriving.
name: Small River

desc:
This smooth plain is composed of dark nutritious soil which supports the many
different kinds of bushes that thrive in this area. This plain is part of the
wide vale that surrounds the river Anduin which flows east of here. The bushy
plain continues westward while in the south there is an open valley covered in
short grass.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
The vegetation here is biased towards short grasses that the cows often graze
upon. The land north of here is covered with short bushes while the valley to
the south seems quite austere, for no grass grows there. A dense-looking
shrubbery that grows south-west of here forms a wall between the field and a
small hut south of the shrubs.
name: Open Valley

desc:
Open land to the north lets northern winds blow across this vale. While the soil
is moist and soft there are no plants able to grow here for the strong winds
sweep all living things away. The ground rises westwards where a small
flimsy-looking hut stands and to the south the valley is covered with bushes.
name: Windswept Valley

desc:
The fields have long since been abandoned, leaving the plants and grasses in a
wild fight for survival. Old and rotting remains of low, wooden fences stand as
silent relics of the former inhabitants of this area. Another reminder of the
past is the flimsy hut located just north of here, slowly degrading and
vanishing as the wild plants climb on it.
name: Field at a Hut

desc:
Small bushes form a row of shrubbery growing next to a flimsy-looking hut that
stands south of here. Some of the shrubs have unfolded their vines and firmly
grasp the wall giving a sense of permanency. A field covered with weeds is
visible to the west while to the north there is a bushy plain. A wide valley
opens out eastward and declines smoothly towards the river Anduin.
name: Shrubbery

desc:
The soft soil is smooth and declines gently eastward where this bushy region
continues. Several small shrubs grow here and there, thriving in this rich soil.
The vegetation changes to the west where it becomes denser because there is more
water available there. Shrubs grow to the south beside a small hut.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Barely reminiscent of its wide, fast-flowing source this small river is hardly
more than a stream in these parts. The even ground gives little aid to propel
the water further east and the riverbanks seem to soak up much of the moisture
to feed the cravings of the surrounding plains. Despite these conditions the
river does continue on eastward.
name: Bend in the Small River

desc:
The river gradually narrows here as it turns gently to the south. The current,
not strong enough to widen its course, slowly pushes the water through the land.
In time these river waters will mingle with those of the great Anduin River,
which lies miles to the east. Some blades of grass float past and a few leaves
drift along on the current.
name: Small River

desc:
The river flows gently along to the east, having expended its energy closer to
its source. All that seems to keep it flowing is the slight downward grade of
the ground, leading it towards the Anduin vale. The surface of the water is
unusually still, giving no hints as to the river's movement. An old dying tree
stands at the southern bank.
name: Silent River

desc:
The plains spread out alongside this small river, accompanying it as it dances
eastwards to join the powerful Anduin on its vast and arduous journey towards
the distant southern lands. Not much grows here, even though the proximity to
the water should create a suitable base for vegetation. A large old tree stands
a few yards away from the southern bank of the river.
name: Large Tree in the Plains

desc:
Huge bushes with massive leaves create a gloomy atmosphere for this region. A
few tall trees with wide spreading lush branches cast shadows upon the
surrounding lands reinforcing the dark ambience. The vegetation to the east
grows sparser since fewer and smaller bushes grow there.
name: Shady Brush

desc:
This field is almost entirely covered by the rampant growth of weeds. They now
occupy the space of other plants which suffer from the strangling the weeds
cause. The lands nearby look more fertile and the plant life in these areas
flourish for they do not suffer from strangling weeds. The brush to the north
differs from the other nearby areas in that it appears very shady with a couple
of huge trees casting shadows about.
name: Weedy Meadow

desc:
This wide valley descends from the west towards a flimsy-looking hut in the
east. The vegetation here is short of stature but very robust with various kinds
of brightly coloured flowers all about. A small spring with crystal-clear water
is located southward and several traces of animals can be seen leading through
here towards the spring.
name: Flourishing Valley

desc:
The ground descends steeply from the west where the edge of the valley can be
seen. The deep, dark brown soil is very nutritious and forms the basis for the
many different kinds of plants growing here. The ground is mostly covered with
grass but a few small bushes and a young birch grove full of thin saplings are
also here.
name: Flourishing Valley

desc:
The grass, over a foot high, covers this flourishing vale between the lush
shrubs and several young birches that grow here. The land rises towards the west
where the edge of this valley is visible. This extensive vale continues to the
distant east where the great Anduin river flows.
name: Fertile Cove

desc:
The area is overshadowed by a pair of ancient trees with huge canopies that
provide shade here and to some brush in the east. The land declines sharply from
the west where the border of the vale is visible. Terrain similar to this region
continues far to the east with various small types of vegetation, like grass and
weeds, growing all around.
name: Shady Vale

desc:
From this point only a faint whispering sound can be heard from the river that
lies to the north. A large oak tree dominates the area, emitting a rotten smell
similar to that of unburied corpses. Some large trees far to the south cast dark
shadows upon much of this bushy plain.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
The river slowly meanders ever onward to the east at a smooth, silent pace.
Occasionally a small fish can be seen leaping out of the water creating a brief
splash, breaking the serene silence. The usual water creatures go about their
business; insects skim the surface, fish swim in the current, and birds swoop
down to pluck a trout from the water. The river is bordered by plains to the
north and south. The ruins of a village can be spotted nearby to the north.
name: Silent River

desc:
Silent and desolate best describe these plains that stretch out as far as the
eye can see. Like a vast brown carpet they welcome travellers to take a
leisurely stroll. However, this featureless area quickly becomes monotonous as
the only break in this bleak landscape is a small collection of collapsed huts,
probably the remains of an old farm slowly becoming as lifeless as the rest of
the surroundings.
name: Barren Plains

desc:
This is the western edge of a wide valley which runs north and south. To the far
east the mighty river Anduin flows in its centre. The lands to the east are
covered with many different kinds of vegetation, most of them flourishing. The
ground slopes sharply down from the edge to the centre of the Vale of Anduin.
The plains to the north appear quite barren.
name: The Valley Edge

desc:
The bushy terrain to the west ends here as the ground becomes flatter and the
bushes slowly die out. Eastwards, an enormous valley opens out and spreads far
to the east where it enfolds the river Anduin. Overall the vale is flourishing
with a large variety of vegetation. A huge rock, some twenty feet high, stands
at the edge of the valley.
name: The Valley Edge

desc:
The river flows in from the east and then continues southward. Near the edges of
the river one can see an occasional elm tree with its roots sticking into the
water to drink. The water flowing over and around the roots causes eddies to
stir up the silt on the riverbed which clouds the water. An occasional piece of
root, or light piece of dirt, floats on the surface.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
The swift water of the Anduin flows from the north and follows a curved route to
the west. A good deal of water sometimes splashes onto the land by the river
bend when the river is running high, making the grass wet and the ground muddy.
The mud from the edges of the riverbank sometimes flows into the river, clouding
up the water and making it impossible to see the bottom.
name: Bend in the Anduin River

desc:
Hidden behind trees and bushes, a small shadowed cave has been dug into the
slope. Twelve feet deep, its entrance is large enough to allow even the tallest
creatures to pass. Scattered on the floor of the cave are the remnants of small
animals, skeletal parts from larger prey and a few pieces of torn fur.
name: A Shadowed Cave

desc:
This poorly constructed cottage, made of splintery planks, has been severely
beaten by the weather. The floor is covered with old filth, much of it in a
decaying state, indicating a lack of recent usage. The roof has several holes
which makes this place a poor shelter on rainy days. There are no windows in
this hut and the only places for light to enter are through the cracks in the
walls and through the doorway when the door is opened.
name: Flimsy Hut

desc:
This small cellar was once a storage area for food and other goods. While the
cool air preserved the foodstuffs for many years they have long since decayed.
Dust has settled deeply over the years this dwelling has been abandoned by its
previous inhabitants. The walls of the cellar are made of clay and the floor is
covered by a few planks.
name: A Hidden Cellar

desc:
The walls of the mound are made out of large, rough stone blocks that were set
into the walls of the mound with great care. Back when they were added the
blocks were tightly fitted but now the cracks between the blocks have widened,
revealing the soil behind. The floor was beaten down and then mixed with small
pebbles. In the centre of the room stands a large, smooth, perfectly rectangular
stone slab. There are no engravings to name who might once have lain on it so
one can only speculate.
name: Inside a Burial Mound

desc:
The air is damp and has an earthy smell to it. The walls and floor consist of
dark, rich soil. Roots of different sizes penetrate through walls like long
fingers as they try and find fresh soil for water and nutrients.
name: Damp Cave

desc:
This was once a tunnel in the earth, but the beams that once supported its
ceiling eventually rotted too much to hold the weight of the earth above and
collapsed. Small pieces of bone peek up from the pile of rubble, showing where a
traveller was crushed. The room is filled with dirt, pieces of broken
ceiling-beams, and small rocks. This passage way leads only to the east now.
name: Collapsed Escape Route

desc:
Here the old escape route turns from south to east. The floor is covered with
debris and rocks, showing where some of the ceiling caved in at one time. In the
eastern wall is a small, dark opening from where a strong smell of decay
emanates.
name: Battered Escape Route

desc:
This low and humid room must once have been a storage chamber judging from the
debris and scraps of storage boxes that lie on the floor. The strong smell of
rot and decay that fills the entire cave-like room comes from where things were
once stored. The ceiling is low; what might have been an exit in it has been
destroyed by the movement of the ground over time. To the west, an opening leads
to a passage away from here.
name: The Storage Chamber

desc:
This is an old tunnel, dug through the earth from north to south. To the south,
the faint sound of water moving about can be heard; northwards there is a deep
and quiet darkness. The walls and ceiling are buttressed by heavy beams of some
dark wood, which seem to have rotted in places, but they are still capable of
supporting the roof.
name: An Escape Route

desc:
This whole room inclines to the north where there is a large wooden door with
metal fittings set in the wall. The room's slope leaves the southern half of the
room flooded with water that at its deepest is only a few feet. The drier parts
of the floor are covered with slightly damp soil. From what can be seen of the
ceiling and walls, past massive spider webs, they appear to be formed of stone
blocks with tangles of roots protruding through them.
name: Flooded Escape Route

desc:
There appear to be walls in every direction except to the east. Vision is
limited to a couple feet but it's possible to feel a small current in the water.
The bottom seems to slope upwards to the east. The only roof that can be seen is
water.
name: Flooded Basement

desc:
The walls of this ancient cellar are made of huge, greyish stone blocks that
were once perfectly joined. Some water trickles in between the blocks; no one
has tried to plug the holes up and no other maintenance seems to have been done
in quite a long time. At the far end there is a pool of water on the wooden
floor. The floorboards are slippery with green moss and the ceiling beams are
covered with spider webs.
name: An Ancient Cellar

desc:
You are inside the belly of a very old tree that is almost completely rotten,
the only thing that is fairly intact is its roots and parts of the branches. At
the top of the tree you can see the sky lying above like a large carpet. You can
also hear the whispers of a river that must be sneaking around somewhere nearby.
name: Inside the Belly of an Old Tree

desc:
The strong scent carried on the air is one of stagnancy and decay. It is similar
to rotting flesh, although no bodies are evident here, or in the area below.
This oak tree is obviously ancient, and these branches show signs of decay,
greatly weakening their support. This part of the tree appears to have died long
ago, and the rest of the tree is sure to follow. Looking down, the ground below
seems incredibly far away; falling from this height could cause serious injury.
name: Rotten Branches

desc:
The Old Forest Road turns sharply, heading south and west. Immediately to the
east, a wide path splits off from the road and heads north. It narrows as it
goes. The Old Forest Road levee sits just north, protecting the road from the
flooding that occurs in a large basin just beyond the road. In the near east,
the gates of a village abruptly halt the road.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
As the Old Forest Road bends from the east to the south, a footpath intersects
the bend and widens. This blurs the edge of the road with the beginning of the
path, making it difficult to distinguish one from the other. A large settlement
has grown up where the Old Forest Road ends in the south, protecting itself
within a wall of brush and trees.
name: Wide Path

desc:
The earth has been worn bare by the footfalls of traders between the northern
settlement and the village south of here. The path narrows and bends to the
east. It winds in the erratic fashion most footpaths take. The unusual thing
about this path is how narrow it becomes, much thinner than a path travelled
with domesticated animals. The path also lacks wagon tracks. This must be a true
footpath.
name: Narrowing Path

desc:
This levee was constructed just to the south of a large basin, too near the Old
Forest Road. If not for this grass-covered hill, the water flooding that
depression would have overtaken the road long ago. Riding north, into the basin,
is not possible. The earth quickly becomes too wet for animals or men to find a
stable foothold. From this elevation, on a clear day, one could easily see the
hills that contain this basin to the north and west or watch the Old Forest Road
in the south, as is winds east to west. A swarm of hornets is coming out of its
nest.
name: Levee near the Old Forest Road

desc:
This levee is just one of many constructed to reign in the flooding of the basin
in the west. Grass spreads from the eastern plains and blankets these man-made
hills in a green wash of colour. As the western and northern slopes descend into
the basin, riding quickly becomes impossible as the earth softens with moisture.
Standing water and fields of mud can be seen covering the land for miles in the
west.
name: On a Levee

desc:
Down the northern slope, a secluded vale is nestled between the surrounding
hills. Down the grass-covered southern and western slopes, the earth turns to a
muddy mush as standing water on the plains creeps up this small outcropping.
Travel southward or westwards is impossible mounted, for the muddy earth offers
no solid footing for man or animal. Travel in that muddy basin would be
difficult at best.
name: Grassy Knoll

desc:
The ground begins to dip down towards the southeast, forming a shallow
depression in the plains between the hills and levees. The earth is soft here,
undoubtedly because of the high moisture content of this soil. The ground gets
softer and wetter as the earth drops off to the east. Travelling in that
direction would be difficult at best, but impossible if one tried to ride there.
name: Slight Depression in the Plains

desc:
Small hills in the west give way to a shallow valley directly to the east of
here. The earth softens as it slopes downhill towards a depression in the
plains. The depression is contained on the east side by hills and man-made
levees in the shape of a crescent moon. Riding towards the east would only be
possible if the ground remained as solid as it is here, but that doesn't appear
to be the case. The grass that is so characteristic of these plains is overcome
by plants that survive in a wet environment further down the depression.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The ground slopes down quickly here to the east. While the elevation change
isn't very drastic, the sudden drop off still makes travel eastward difficult
and impossible if mounted. From this position, a large pool of water a mile or
so in the east can be seen spreading out and merging with the earth for many
miles to the east and north. To the south, the levees that protect the Old
Forest Road from water damage stand over the depression in the plains.
name: Large Basin

desc:
Vibrant, green grass covers this area until it reaches the Old Forest Road
directly to the south. The earth rises slightly to the south, elevating the road
above the surrounding plains. In the northeast, a large basin stretches out for
many miles and is filled with water. Travel in that direction could prove
difficult.
name: Lush Grass

desc:
Just north of the Old Forest Road, the ground suddenly drops off towards a large
field of stagnant water that is immediately to the north. The slope is
traversable by unmounted travellers, but still very difficult. The water has
made the earth soft and very likely to collapse under too much weight. From this
vantage, plants and animals not common to the Vale can be seen in the water.
name: Edge of the Mud Plains

desc:
Just north of the Old Forest Road, the earth drops off towards the north. It
leads into what appears to be a flooded basin. Many miles to the northeast,
hills and man-made levees have successfully trapped the water leaving it
stagnant in that depression. Travel down the slope seems easy enough to do on
foot but riding down would not be possible as the earth quickly becomes too
saturated for mounts to keep their footing.
name: Edge of the Mud Plains

desc:
The ground is rocky and somewhat abrupt in this area, and it rolls more and more
to the east, vale after hillock and hill after valley, while the earth beneath
rises gradually as it moves away from the stony banks of the Anduin. At the top
of one of the hills to the east, you can see an old wooden building, most
probably a shelter for weary travellers.
name: Low Rocky Hills

desc:
A small stream springs suddenly out of the rocks that make up the hillock,
forming a tiny but clear reservoir in a little hollow, and then quickly
disappears into the stones down the hillside. Brooks like this are common in
these lands, and the vale's abundant rain filters through the rocky earth to
then gush forth from a myriad of cracks and holes.
name: A Small Brook

desc:
These grassy hills are the worn remnants of sharp, rocky tears in the plains.
Their history is told by the small pebbles that litter the grass, telltale signs
of the erosion that has occurred here over the eons. Now grass creeps up from
the nearby fields, covering the rock and blanketing the landscape in lush green.
name: Worn Hills

desc:
Lush, green grass blankets the landscape in all directions. Here the grass
reaches so high that your tracks are quickly swallowed by the plains. On a
closer inspection, small wildflowers can be seen popping up between the blades
of grass. Anyone stopping long enough to notice the small flowers might also
catch the stronger smell of flowers on a southerly breeze.
name: High Grass

desc:
The nearby Anduin River makes these plains rich and fertile. Thick green grass
covers the plains for miles around but the occasional wildflower can be seen
springing up between the blades of grass. A few miles south, the ground begins
to slope downwards towards a large basin, a depression in the plains. In the
distant south, a large road can be seen winding to the east and south.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
In the south and east, the ground begins a gradual slope downwards into a
depression in the plains. Heading either east or farther west, a traveller would
encounter impressive hills, much more taxing to ascend than the worn hills in
the north. To the south, the ground slopes down into a shallow depression that
extends for many miles. In that basin, stagnant water has collected and forms a
treacherous, muddy mix that would be very difficult to traverse.
name: Level Plains

desc:
The ground slopes downward to the south from here. Underfoot, the earth gives a
bit under the weight of its travellers, as it is saturated with water and softer
than the ground to the north. Down the southward slope, the earth seems to get
wetter and in the incline more treacherous. A group of hills and levees, in a
crescent shape, stretch to the east and south and form a barrier for the
depression directly to the south.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
The earth takes a subtle downward slide towards the south. The farther south one
travels, the more saturated the earth becomes with water, and the more
treacherous and difficult the southward descent becomes. Riding a mount down
this seemingly gentle slope quickly becomes impossible. A group of hills and
levees in a crescent shape stretch to the east and south and form a barrier for
the depression directly to the south.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
This high, grassy hill offers an excellent view of this area's plains and vales.
Immediately below, towards the south, brown wetlands stretch out until the land
rises near the levees protecting the Old Forest Road. Travel in the south would
not be possible mounted. Down the eastern slope, a small, secluded grove sits
surrounded by hills in every direction.
name: On a High Hill

desc:
Small trees spring up under the protective branches of mature trees only to be
snuffed out by lack of soil in this small vale. Life is plentiful here. Green
moss covers the earth because the sun that grass would so desperately need does
not penetrate the leaves of the trees. Wild flowers, accustomed to surviving in
less than perfect conditions, grow abundantly in the vale, providing a soft
cushion for weary travellers.
name: Secluded Glen

desc:
Old and worn, these hills seem to have been here since the creation of Arda, and
to have endured every cataclysm in its history. The oak wood that grows over
their rounded outlines seem to be almost as old, formed by great trees with
thick, grey barks and broad and curved branches hanging from the trunks. Perhaps
these very millennial oaks gave shade to the first Eldar while they rested
before crossing the Misty Mountains, in their long trip to the Undying Lands.
name: Oak-covered Hill

desc:
A large rock stands lonely at the top of this hill. Even though the features are
coarse, and the surfaces smooth and worn by the effect of rain and wind, it
unmistakably resembles the figure of a great bear, rising on its huge legs in a
menacing attitude. Two knobs on the rock take the shape of the arms, and, in the
upper part, the stone looks rounded, except for the projecting snout. Some
cracks even vaguely recall the beast's ears and claws. Whether someone sculpted
this statue, or it is only a miracle of nature, remains unknown.
name: The Bear Rock

desc:
You are now at the southern border of an impressive dale, one that is not run of
the mill, but that stands out of the many ones that give this land its name: the
Anduin Vales. To the north, the valley gets wider, as if the hillsides had
withdrawn respectfully from its path. But here, the vale dies at the mouth of
what looks like a tight glen, that quickly disappears, wandering into the abrupt
faces of two hills.
name: Mouth of a Small Gully

desc:
A small wooden building, old and worn by the years, stands at the top of the
hill. A short, earthy incline leads to the entrance, as if demanding a last
effort from the traveller, and even further inside, for the door disappeared
long ago, or perhaps the refuge never had one. All along the oaken planks, the
grains of the wood mingle with the stains caused by the rain, forming a mosaic
that covers the walls like a natural tapestry. The intense scent of wet wood
fills the air, but all in all, the house has a comfortable look.
name: An Old Refuge in the Hills

desc:
In this land of hills and valleys, this is undoubtedly the roughest area. The
ground is rocky and rugged, almost devoid of vegetation, except for some scrub
that grows among the rocks. The eastern face of the hill looks particularly
dangerous, as a narrow gully has formed along the bottom of the tight vale.
There, the land falls away steeply downwards, wandering among large rocks and
crags.
name: A Hill over a Narrow Gully

desc:
These worn remnants of sharp, rocky tears in the plains have been smoothed by
time and weather. Only the small pebbles underfoot give any sign of the mighty
outcroppings that once stood here. Now grass from the nearby fields covers the
rock and the land in a sea of green.
name: Worn Hills

desc:
Suddenly, wildflowers engulf the landscape, totally choking the life from the
grass so abundant in these plains. The green, that so overwhelmed the landscape
in every direction, has surrendered to the colour these wild weeds offer. In the
midst of so many flowers, it might occur to one that the abrupt change in plant
life was too sudden. This field may have been planted by someone.
name: In the Wildflowers

desc:
The earth levels out here before rising to the east and dropping off to the
south. Heading either east or farther west, a traveller would encounter
impressive hills, much more taxing to ascend than the worn hills in the north.
To the south, the ground slopes down into a shallow depression that spans for
many miles. In that depression, stagnant water has collected and forms a
treacherous, muddy mix that would be very difficult to traverse.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This small knoll leads to a large hill to the east and falls towards a
depression in the south. Green grass covers this slight protrusion in the
landscape, dotted with the occasional wild flower and errant bee. Northwards,
the occurrences of wildflowers increase, heading towards what looks like a field
of wildflowers.
name: Grassy Knoll

desc:
Two large hills, one to the east the other to the south, share this incline as
their base. In either of those directions, travellers should prepare for a bit
of a climb, as these hills are rather steep. But here, the soft, green grass
eases tired feet. It offers a thick cushion over the hard ground. To the far
north and west, the Anduin Vales sprawl for miles.
name: Soft Incline

desc:
In-between a small grove of trees and a wildflower field, a small brush thicket
has sprung up, snuffing out the grass with its thick vines and bushes. Easily
traversable on foot, mounted riders would not be able to navigate this stretch
of land. In the north and south, hills rise above the plains, hiding vales
between them.
name: Brush Thicket

desc:
Leading into a small brush thicket in the west, this elm grove offers excellent
haven for those too weary from the sun to travel any further. Mature elms reach
high enough to give small animals protection from land predators. But younger
trees have low enough leaves that wandering plant-eaters could feed here, if
needed.
name: Small Wild Grove

desc:
Above a small elm grove, this hill has done an excellent job of withstanding the
punishment of time. Unlike smaller hills in the north, this one still bares
jagged outcroppings and steep slopes. Grass covered, it is not barren of small
bushes or wildflowers. Without doubt this is a large hill but not as large as
the one that lies to the south-west. Immediately below this hill, a small glen
has prospered in the valley.
name: Atop a Hill

desc:
The earth smoothes and levels here for a short while. In every direction, small
hills give rise to larger ones, hiding the vales this land is famous for in
between them. Just to the south, a small, wild grove of trees sits protected by
a steep hill further south. In the east, a faint path widens as it leads to a
small settlement.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Every now and then, as you look around, the landscape seems to blur for a brief
instant. And then you feel carried to the distant ocean, far away westwards
beyond the Lindon coasts. At times, the hills shape into huge, threatening waves
that loom above you; at other times, the swinging of the grass in the wind turns
into the scene of a calm, peaceful sea that quickly fades away. Perhaps Ulmo's
influence is still powerful in the Great River, and it extends to the
surrounding lands.
name: Rolling Ground

desc:
This abrupt hill seems to rise above the surrounding ones. The ground is rough
and rocky, and the grass scarce, and in its place stones of different sizes lie
everywhere. The earth is uneven, with steep slopes, and demands additional
effort to walk, the scattered rocks only making it worse. But the western
hillside commands your attention, descending almost vertically down to a narrow
glen that makes its way through the feet of the hills. To the north, the figure
