of a bear stands at the top of a hill and, a bit further away, you can discern
the hillsides of a majestic valley.
name: A Particularly Steep Hill

desc:
The sunlight hardly reaches the bottom of this tight glen, along which some
brook flowed long ago. Winding whimsically, it follows the narrow path between
the abrupt walls of the hills surrounding it. Even though both walls are steep,
the eastern one looks particularly sheer, as it plummets down from the top. The
mouth of a cave appears here and there in the craggy hillside, most of the times
too high to reach it. However, a shadowy cavern, deep black and mysterious, goes
into the hill, only a few steps away.
name: A Narrow Gully

desc:
Between two unimpressive hills lies this small, brush-covered glen. In earlier
days, the hills surrounding this tiny glade were mighty projections from the
earth. In those days, venturing into this glen would have been a difficult task.
But now, the gentle slopes offer an easy entrance to this valley and steeper
ones to the north.
name: An Open Glen

desc:
Flowers of every shape, size and colour surround you, some as tall as a man,
others no taller than a rabbit. Most can be found throughout the land, but some
are so unusual that they live nowhere but in these fertile plains. In the east,
a small brush thicket has grown up in the shadow of a large hill in the south.
name: Wildflower Field

desc:
From this vantage, the lay of the southern lands is placed before you. Hills,
worn smooth by time, frame the vast sea of green grass that covers the plains. A
large depression in the southwest houses a swampy mud-filled plain, an area that
most inhabitants of this land avoid. To the north, however, a very steep hill
completely blocks the view, leaving travellers who wish to know what lies in
that direction with only one choice: keep climbing.
name: Gradual Northern Rise

desc:
A rocky cavern opens before you. Its uneven ground contrasts its high ceiling.
So much so, that you can guess, more than see, what lies behind the deep shadows
above your head. The rocks that form the cave are craggy, with sharpened
projections and twisted shapes, and even those with the smoother surfaces are
dangerously slippery because of the moisture. This makes it necessary to advance
more slowly, even testing your footing to avoid falling. But, as if unaware of
the world outside, the atmosphere remains cold, though bearable, throughout the
whole year.
name: A Cold, Wet Grotto

desc:
The atmosphere inside the building is cold and very humid, but at least its four
walls and ceiling protect from the sometimes icy wind and the rain. Opposite the
entrance, a ramshackle table constitutes the only furniture. Small dark marks
here and there reveal the existence of leaks in the roof, that, for the rest,
appears to be in a reasonably good condition. In the most sheltered corner, a
little circle of tiny stones, together with the soot- covered walls, and,
mainly, the ash-coloured floor, make it clear that it would be the perfect spot
to light a fire.
name: Inside the Shelter

desc:
A wide vista opens up for a person on this tall rock, making it possible for
many to see the river Anduin flowing peacefully in the far east. The sides of
the rock are sheer except the western edge where descent seems possible. The
surface of the rock is covered with the excrement of birds.
name: On Top of the Huge Rock

desc:
This dim cave with black, deep shadows lies hidden in the bowels of the hill.
However, there are evident signs of animal life in it: hair shreds hanging from
the rocky projections, some of them at a suspiciously excessive height;
unidentifiable remains of food; an abnormally high temperature; and also the
unmistakable smell of living beings in an enclosed place. Undoubtedly, this
gloomy cavern constitutes the lair of some great beast.
name: A Shadowy Lair

desc:
Tall grass, small rocks, and scattered bushes cover this small hill. Standing on
its top it is possible to get a good view of the surrounding land. A road runs
around this hill to the north and west. Beyond the road to the north is a sea of
grass. Down the slope to the east is a wild patch of weeds.
name: A Small Hill by the Bend

desc:
The Old Forest Road bends around the small hill to the east of here. Westwards
fields surround the road along with a group of trees to the northwest. It is
possible to travel east to the top of the small hill.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
A heap of rags, rotten vegetables and pieces of wood lies here in the middle of
an open field, just south of a bend in the Old Forest Road. Upon closer
examination, even broken tools and containers are noticeable, but it seems
unlikely that there is anything of use under all the junk. A small wagon with a
broken wheel leans to the pile of garbage; all this looks like leftovers from a
merchant or a group of settlers.
name: A Garbage Pile

desc:
Recent cart traffic through these fields has left pairs of deep, narrow tracks
into the soft ground. The tracks rise up from a ditch in the south and continue
towards the Old Forest Road in the north. A small footpath appears from the
bushes in the west, joining the cart tracks at an opening, which is surrounded
by short trees. At the edge of the opening, some chopped logs lie on the ground,
and the grass is burnt in some places.
name: Cart Tracks through the Field

desc:
Fallen trees from the clump of trees to the north have almost blocked this road.
The logs appear to have been here for quite a while since the sun has made them
greyish and hard. Carts would have an extremely hard time trying to negotiate
their way past these logs, but a person or their mount would have little problem
jumping over them.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Bushes and high grass line the fringe of the Old Forest Road. Occasionally a
bush finds a place in the middle of the road to set its roots. Northeast of here
a large clump of trees can be seen and further to the east a small hill stands.
To the west, the road bends to follow the Anduin River's bank north towards its
source for a short distance.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
As the road bends here it runs north along the riverbank to where it makes
another turn and east towards a small hill. Grass has taken root in the old cart
tracks that scar the road and bushes of several kinds surround the road.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
A neat row of thorny bushes starts here, and continues for about half a mile to
the east. The hedge is impenetrable in most places, but there are a few gaps
where passing through would not be difficult even for a rider. In the north,
grassy fields separate the hedgerow from the Old Forest Road; a road that lies
partially hidden from view behind the bushes of various sizes and shapes that
grows in between.
name: A Hedgerow South of the Road

desc:
A pair of round, smooth hillocks stand out in this treeless meadow of weeds and
flowers. Molehills dot the slopes on all sides; the loose soil coupled with the
absence rocks here seems ideal for digging tunnels and hovels. A dirt path leads
around the hillocks towards the Old Forest Road in the north, while to the
southeast it disappears into a shrubbery.
name: Low Hillocks

desc:
A babbling brook runs through a hollow in the plains here. The streambed is
rocky and much wider than the stream itself; travelling on the bottom of the
hollow is somewhat hard compared to the gentle, grass-covered slopes in the
north and in the south. Thin-branched bushes stand out among the rocks on both
sides of the stream, and occasionally signs of small birds living among them can
be noticed.
name: A Trickling Stream

desc:
A verdant field of clover and small flowers spreads out from here towards the
muddy furrow of a stream in the southeast. In the summertime, the local bees
must find an endless supply of nectar from the round flowers of the clover;
there are also plants that rabbits and deer might dig up to eat during long
winters. Some droppings lie on the ground, but whatever animal left them seems
to have scurried away; almost no bushes to hide in grow along the paths leading
through this field.
name: A Field of Clover

desc:
The slender stems of flowering plants standing in this verdant field undulate
slightly whenever there is a breeze. Many of them are spreading their seeds over
the field; their petals have dried up and fallen away, but the time has not
wiped out their colour entirely. Large bluish petals lying on the ground are
slowly turning brown, and there are also some dry sunflowers that still have
seeds left for birds to eat. Clusters of tiny flowers are thriving in the shade
under a lonely elm standing in the fields.
name: A Field of Mixed Flowers

desc:
The thick grass growing here is wet, especially near the bank in the north,
where the waters of Anduin occasionally splash over the embankment. Clear
puddles cover the grass in depressions, and smaller ones are slowly forming to
fill in any tracks left on the soft ground. The riverbank to the west is covered
by small, grey pebbles, which have turned smooth and round over the years they
have spent on the bottom of the river.
name: Riverside Fields

desc:
A stream flowing into these muddy fields from the east has carved a wide ditch
into the soft ground. Steep slopes lead away from the stream in the west and in
the north, where some cart tracks can be seen leading down to the stream and
continuing over to the cultivated fields in the south. The mud looks somewhat
sinking around the tracks, but the ground in the east seems rockier, and there
are even places where the water of the stream is clear enough to drink.
name: Crossing a Ditch

desc:
High hay and small bushes grow here and there, while a carpet of thick, tangled
grass covers most of the ground. Some of the dry plants among the grass have
been crushed by something that recently passed through here, spreading their
large, flat seeds to the ground. In the west, the grass suddenly disappears and
a field of sand continues down to the river.
name: Lush Grasses

desc:
This is all that remains of an old homestead that was burned to the ground a
long time ago. Cauliflowers, turnips, and celery grow in what appears to be an
old garden. North of here a road runs across the plains, and to the east the
Anduin River flows.
name: Razed Homestead

desc:
Bushes of various shapes and sizes dominate the landscape. An occasional burst
of buttercups gives this ragged land a bit of lustre. To the west, the bushes
are gradually replaced by trees. To the north the bushes continue.
name: Brushwood

desc:
The soil is much rockier than in the forest west of here, which seems to inhibit
the growth of the dense forest. A few tall and stout trees grow here but bushes
dominate this edge of the forest. Several large and sharp rocks lie scattered
about.
name: Dense Forest Border

desc:
Tall trees with stout branches form a thick and vivid forest that provides
shelter for the animals living here. The undergrowth looks very dense and is
formed by a large variety of plants. Different kinds of spoor cover the soft
ground providing proof of active life in the region.
name: Dense Mixed Forest

desc:
This spacious forest consists of a variety of trees all with large leaves that
form a very thick-looking canopy. The lower sections of some trees have been
gnawed by animals leaving bits and pieces of bark on the ground. The forest
grows denser in the east and the closest edge of this forest is somewhere to the
west.
name: Sparse Mixed Forest

desc:
This bushy region forms the border between the forest that spreads eastward and
the grassy lands to the west. The mellow soil that nourishes the bushes can
easily be imprinted by the tracks of various animals as they pass through this
region.
name: Small Bushes

desc:
Though short grass and a few small bushes still manage to cover this plain, many
of them have been trampled by animals. Most of the moss that once grew here has
wilted although a few tussocks of it still remain. The grasslands continue to
the west and north while to the east the land begins to be dominated by bushes.
name: Grassland

desc:
The land here is entirely covered by grass with a few small bushes and richly
coloured flowers pushing up between the stalks. A few low mounds are scattered
about and blanketed with a moss. The terrain has more bushes to the west but the
grass here continues into the east and north.
name: Grassland

desc:
When sun or moon shines the reflected light plays in the fields. It jumps from
straw to straw, changing their hues as it goes. The sway of the grass helps,
making the scenery even more spectacular. Beneath this, insects have their homes
among the gravel and dirt, digging tunnels and feeding on whatever they can
claim.
name: Blisters of Green

desc:
The grass rolls out in all directions as a carpet covering the land. An
occasional rock or lonesome tree can be spotted, but very little disturbs the
rule of the grass. Deep within the green tangles white and yellow flowers
struggle to spring up and reach for the beams of the sun.
name: Rolling Greens

desc:
The green tangles hold a firm grip of the earth, letting few or none of the
beams from the sun reach down to their roots. Close to the ground there is
little growth, except for the close-packed stems of grass reaching for the sky.
Gazing out over the fields flowers of white and yellow dot the green, and in the
west a drier landscape reaches out.
name: Rampant Grasslands

desc:
Here the ground follows a gentle descent to the south. Streaks of shallow
ditches run across the land letting the water flow towards the lower lands in
the south. The grass here uses this water to flourish in green, and along the
sides of the shallows white flowers grow closely in patches.
name: Gentle Wet Slope

desc:
These green fields are spotted with moss and lichen, as the rows of hay grow
thinner, decaying in the soggy ground. To the south a sharp descent begins the
ruling of the Gladden Fields. At this descent a grey type of sturdy grass grows
tall and strong, with roots reaching far down in the soft ground.
name: Border of the Gladden Fields

desc:
Moss and lichen form complicated patterns in varying colours over the undulating
ground. Their intricate figures are covered in a thick mist, floating a couple
of feet above the ground. In the north and west the borders of the Gladden run
sharply across the land. In these parts sturdy grey grass grows high above the
mossy earth, serving as the harsh keeper of the marshes.
name: Inner Borders of the Gladden Fields

desc:
Soft moss flows over the landscape into the south and west. The marshes reach
out as far as the eye can see, covered in an apparent mist close to the ground.
The stench of rotten grass and decay lies distinctly in the air. The ancient
shores of the Gladden run in the north and east, spotted with grey grass that
bends into the wet ground. A sharp edge of slippery grass and turf makes it
impossible to move to the east while the marshes smoothly connect with the
fields in the north.
name: Outskirts of the Gladden Fields

desc:
Crawling in from the north-east branches and tangles are desperately reaching
for a hold in the soft ground. All that meets them here though is moist and
muddy earth, and the woody stems quickly decay covered in moss and lichen. In
the south the marshes of Gladden Fields stretch out. Grey grass grows in narrow
rows on the edge, flourishing in the moss.
name: Edge of the Marshes

desc:
Shallow natural furrows run from north to south across the green fields.
Occasional rains fill them with water travelling to the south. There is however
a constant supply of water in these trenches as they are fed with dew and mist
from the endless fields in the north. Venturing upwards to the east takes the
traveller into a maze of tangles towards a sharp slope in the fields.
name: Thriving Slope

desc:
Flourishing green grass stretches out, flowing over the fields. Small bushes and
tangles snare the land to the east, but in this area the swaying grass has a
firm grip of the land. Natural shallow ditches run across the fields in a
disordered pattern. Most of these paths are dried out, with only wet mud to show
for the water that once ran there. Even during rains, the ground is thirsty
enough to not let any water flow away. To the east the path is blocked by a
tangle of brown branches.
name: Rich Rows of Hay

desc:
Between the stems of the green grass, snakes, rats and other small animals may
go about their business. The hay rattles occasionally in the wind or when
disturbed by something deep in the thick green. These lands seem satisfied with
water and sun, flourishing in their growth.
name: Whirling Rows of Grass

desc:
Here, as in the fields around, the grass grows in hundred different hues of
green. All straws of the hay have their unique shades like swaying individuals.
But growing together as they do the result is a rainbow of greens covering the
land. Brown herbs and yellow and white flowers can be spotted within the deep
sea of grass by a keen eye.
name: Blistering Hues of Green

desc:
Here the landscape is covered in hues of green, spotted with streaks of white
and yellow flowers. To the east and south, the ground becomes rougher and
further away tangles of wood and snares spread out. In this area, though, the
undulating grass still rules firmly, covering the earth with its ordered rows of
green stems. A steep hill covered in tangles blocks further travel southeast.
name: Undulating Hays of Green

desc:
Herbs and flowers fight for the their existence beneath the swaying sea of green
hay. Although they can hardly be spotted they fill the air with all kinds of
scents, some earthy, some fresh, some thick, some casual. The herbs have come
together in small patches, creating islands of low brown grass.
name: Flourishing Cover

desc:
A rich cover protects the earth, shielding it from rain and wind. The green
grass serves as food source for numerous hungry animals that pass these fields
on their way to the refreshing water of the Anduin. The grass rattles whenever
light breezes travel over the lands.
name: Dawning Greens

desc:
Close to the north and east, birches are scattered throughout the fields. These
lonesome pillars serve as a wall, hindering the wind from ruling freely. In the
cover of this wall the dust and gravel lies firmly on the ground beneath a
jungle of green straws that seldom have to bend in the power of moving air.
name: Slumbering Fields

desc:
Short, perky bushes grow here in some places while only a few trees remain of
the forest in the north. Together with rocks of various sizes, they provide
cover for small animals in the otherwise bare fields of grass. The great river
lies somewhere to the southeast, where the ground begins to slope gently towards
the riverbed.
name: Sparse Bushes among the Rocks

desc:
The broken remains of a wooden fence surround this old pasture, where shrubs and
small trees have taken over. The grass covering the ground is lush and seems
perfect for domestic animals to graze on. Still, no signs of cattle can be found
here, except for the bleached skull of a cow that sits tucked between two rocks.
The field continues all the way down to the bank of the river in the east.
name: An Overgrown Pasture

desc:
Sticks and branches litter the ground in this brushwood near the riverbed. Some
open ways lead west and north through the dense shrubs here, making it possible
for a rider to follow the course of the river. The paths bend and twist,
stopping anyone from seeing very far, as the shrubbery seems to get thicker at
every turn.
name: Riverside Thicket

desc:
Soft hay covers the wide fields here, partly hiding any tracks that could have
been left in the muddy ground. Slender stems of taller plants sway gently
whenever a breeze passes through; no shelter from the wind can be found in any
direction. In the southeast, some bushes stand near the river.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
The ocean of green so apparent in the surrounding fields, is here spotted with
tangles and shrubs. In the middle of these lakes of brush rocks and stones lie,
serving as the little cover the bushes need to get a hold of the land. By every
stone and every rock, tangles and branches grow close to the sides, and then
spread out into the greens.
name: Tangled Fields

desc:
Here the shrubs and tangles grow thick. Rocks and large stones are also a common
sight. Their presence shapes the landscape into a dense brush. The flora changes
with the diminishing grass, into more brown herbs, instead of the colourful
flowers so common in the surrounding fields. The composition of this immensely
tangled hill makes travelling westwards impossible while its smooth flow towards
the river Anduin almost encourages venturing in that direction.
name: Bushes in the Field

desc:
In the east numerous trees are spread out from the forest in the horizon. They
have attempted to claim this land, but the soft ground sick with water and mud
has rendered it impossible. Rotten stems and moss-covered trunks tell stories of
unfortunate attempts of trees settling. Only a few have managed to send their
roots through the moss into healthy soil hidden beneath the mire.
name: Trees in the Marshes

desc:
By the look of this confusing landscape the grey and green mosses have played in
the sinks of water, resting their soft bodies against dead branches and shallow
rocks. The result is an obscure pattern of undulating mire making no attempt at
an ordered growth.
name: Swirling Mosses

desc:
On the ground rotting leaves and branches are spread out between the peaks of
mosses and lichen. A struggling forest is located in the south, and these
branches are the results of the attempts of the forest at spreading into the wet
marshes. The steep border of the Gladden climbs to the fields above. Along the
sides of the border grey grass with sharp stems covers the earth and moss. From
beyond this grass the flow of the mighty Anduin can be heard.
name: Ancient Shore of the Gladden

desc:
This patch of trees has broken in on the soggy grounds of the Gladden Fields. At
its borders the earth has become too wet and soft for the sturdy roots to grip
the soil. It serves as the borders of this small forest. In the east a moist
field stretches out between the trees and the Anduin, and some of the young
pines have started to spread in that direction, extending the edge of the wood.
name: A Patch of Trees

desc:
When the wind comes from the east these fields are sprayed with a gentle mist
from the Anduin. Even as the wind seize, the dew holds firmly to the swaying
grass, with never-ending moisture. In the west a mossy wall of trees grows
closely together, clinging to each other in the mossy ground as if to avoid
sinking into the earth. Some of the trees have immigrated into these fields, but
the rest quickly turn away in the north and south.
name: Moist Plain at the Anduin

desc:
Cramped in between the Anduin and the Gladden Fields, these fields are covered
in a gentle mist. The roaring of the Great River rushes over the plains, and a
strong scent of decay flows in from the west. Yellow flowers grow in patches,
and in the west sturdy grey grass decorates the border of the marshes. A sharp
drop into the marsh makes travelling this way impossible.
name: Watery Fields

desc:
Yellow patches of gentle flowers spot the land, spreading out in the grass that
still stands tall in the soft ground. The rushing sound of the Anduin flows in
over the lands, and its sparkling flow glitters in the south-east. Sturdy grey
stems of grass grow in row after row at the border of the Gladden in the west,
and a sharp slippery decline leads into the soggy marsh.
name: Damp Fields of Hay

desc:
Dead rotting branches of the brush reach in from the north. The ground has
become soft and soggy, and unusable for the woody stems of bushes and tangles
that try to complete their ruling of the lands. Orange grass still sways gently
in the plains, but the earth is spotted with moss and lichen.
name: Dying Tangles

desc:
On this peak in the undulating fields a noticeable part of the ground is covered
with short brown shrubs and tangles, which make riding through here difficult.
Their woody stems are spotted with delicate green leaves, desperately holding on
to the bark. At the sharp border of the brush-covered hill and the green swaying
hay in the west, intricate patterns of grass and tangles are formed close to the
ground. Higher up the grass stands proudly above the bushes, majestically
swaying when the wind blows.
name: Shallow Tangle

desc:
All around small sturdy tangles and shrubs cover the earth. The hollows beneath
the branches, close to the ground, make perfect homes for snakes and lizards.
They also make the terrain rather dangerous for those travelling on horseback.
The brush spreads out over a hill with sharp impassable edges in the north and
west, but gently levels in the east and south.
name: Tangles across the Grasslands

desc:
Green and flourishing, these fields lie snugly in a valley at the turn of the
Anduin in the east and south. The Great River's rushing water echoes over the
land. The ground is soft and wet, caused by the close relation to the flowing
water, but the level earth serves as good growth place for grass and flowers.
Close in the north a large gathering of rocks and debris has been gathered next
to a few sturdy pines, forming a moderately concealed hovel in the field.
Peaking out from the top, a column of rocks and crudely cut boards form a watch
tower, not higher then the trees next to it, but protected with a slanting roof
and walls in all directions.
name: Concealed Camp at the Anduin

desc:
Not far in the east the gentle murmur of the Anduin current echoes through the
land. These fields are once in a while filled with the rising water as the
bending river sometimes cannot follow its destined path. Rain and dew also
choose these fields as a course towards the flowing water of the Anduin. This
results in a soft and muddy field sparsely filled with swaying hay and the
shrubs reaching in from the north rotting away in the mist. In the south a rocky
mound blocks the path along the river.
name: Misty Shores

desc:
The soil is soft and wet, spotted with moss and lichen. The rain that falls
occasionally drains to the south and east ending up in the Anduin. Shrubs and
tangles reach in from the north and west.
name: Wet Brush

desc:
Low shrubs reaching to the knees of a full-grown man, cover parts of this
landscape. The tall swaying grass is however not ready to ease its firm grip of
the fields. In the mixture of brush and orange grass, flowers and herbs take the
opportunity to flourish and fill the air with sweet scents.
name: Patches of Shrubs

desc:
The tangles and shrubs stretch in from the north crawling over the land as if
alive. Between the bushes moss and flowers struggle for a piece of earth. A
scent of wood and resin fills the misty air. In the southeast the glittering
shores of the Great River sparkle when it curls through the fields.
name: Brush-filled Fields

desc:
The tangles of shrubs crawling in from the north meet their sudden end here. The
far shores of the Anduin are soft and drenched with moist. Here the roots and
life of the brush quickly rot away, loosening their grip in the soil. The stems
that have survived here are spotted with moss and lichen. Instead the fields are
patterned with fragile straws of green grass, and small yellow flowers.
name: Rotting Stems of the Brush

desc:
Almost no trees grow on this bank of the river, whereas a hedgerow clearly
outlines the riverbed in the southeast. The ground slopes gently away from the
riverbed, turning from an undulating meadow of weeds into a flat field. Large
rocks break the hedge in some places, but climbing up on them would not give a
much better view over the river.
name: A Hedge Lining the Riverbank

desc:
This beach reaches further into the river than the grassy banks; it looks like
most of it would be under the water if the surface was a few feet higher. The
river has left bundles of loose branches, leaves and hay on the beach, where
they slowly rot under the sun. A dead trunk sticks out of the sand several yards
away from the water, leaning towards the ground. The droppings on the trunk
indicate that birds must often sit there, watching the river.
name: A Sandy Beach at the Riverbank

desc:
Thick layers of mud along with tufts of hardy grasses cover the ground here. A
narrow stream flows in from the east and carries fine sand and clay into the
Anduin, clouding the water near the bank. The furrow of the stream widens as it
turns into a muddy ditch farther inland, where it continues to the east past
grassy fields and meadows of flowers.
name: Muddy Bank of the Anduin

desc:
Dry weeds and woody stems snap and crackle whenever anything moves around in
these fields. Some dead flowers lie on the ground, and their rotting petals
still show hints of crimson and blue. Tall grasses grow in the south, where a
slope leads away from the riverside into a rocky area. Short bushes outline the
course of an overgrown trail leading east and west.
name: A Field of Weeds and Flowers

desc:
Some bushes and tall trees grow near the riverbank here, partly obscuring the
view of the old structure of a stone building. It might well have once been a
working water mill; it is built close to the river, and the stream near the
rocky bank seems strong enough to turn a pair of millstones. It looks like there
was once a trail leading to the east from the yard of the building, but the
ground is now covered in weeds and short bushes.
name: Old Ruins of a Water Mill

desc:
Clusters of blueberries grow among the rocks and short birches here. The ground
is hard and covered by dry leaves, sticks and splinters of wood which form neat
lines along the ant trails in the undergrowth. The highest point in these dry
fields, an outcrop of bare rock, is surrounded by a carpet of moss and stunted
conifer trees.
name: A Patch of Blueberries

desc:
A carpet of grass and tiny flowers covers the ground here, just above the cliffs
that border the riverbed of the Anduin. The ridge starts from the river's edge
and continues to the east, where the vegetation seems to get taller. One could
have a good look over the river from this open field, but one would also be in
danger of being spotted oneself.
name: Riverside Fields above a Cliff

desc:
The small dirt path running through the fields and shrubberies here does not
look like it was made by animals. After following it for a while, a pair of boot
prints, preserved in the hardened mud, can be noticed. Their size is quite
small, but the tracks are too old to tell who passed through here. In the
northwest, lush fields spread out towards the river, while in the opposite
direction tall bushes dominate the landscape.
name: A Footpath through the Fields

desc:
Round shrubs with large leaves grow in tightly packed groups here and there,
covering this dry field. The ground declines slightly to the southeast, but
moving that way seems as hard as going in any other direction; the paths amidst
the shrubbery twist and bend as they lead through the narrow openings between
bushes. A wider path, covered in grass and loose stones, leads through the field
as it follows the direction of the slope.
name: A Shrub-covered Field

desc:
The bank of the river has turned into a landslide where roots and vegetation
failed to hold the sand in place. In many spots the grassy mat suddenly ends at
the waters edge, and a steep embankment of loose soil leads down to the river
instead. A small path follows the course of the river here, and then disappears
into the grass.
name: Steep Banks of the Anduin

desc:
Small ponds and puddles of water break the monotony of these wet plains near the
banks of the Anduin. Clusters of irises and reeds thrive in the shallow waters,
and tall weeds cover the higher grounds. A stretch of rocky fields farther away
from the river offers an easier route through the plains, reaching from the
riverside in the north to the sloping grounds in the south.
name: Wetlands near the River

desc:
This secluded valley seems to be teeming with life; here and there one can catch
glimpses of small animals' tracks and tunnels amidst the thriving undergrowth.
Starting from a spring near the ridge in the north, a small stream runs down to
the bottom of the valley, where it quickly soaks into the ground; there it
serves to nourish the thick clusters of various plants. Rocky fields separate
this area of lush vegetation from the riverside wetlands in the west.
name: A Small Valley

desc:
Here, a gentle slope rises towards the high hills in the south. Stunted trees
and bushes grow in some places, but there is a clear line of sight almost all
the way down to the wide plains in the north and east. Moving along the hillside
seems rather easy, and many paths lie open to travellers; paths leading past
rocky outcroppings and dry fields.
name: Gently Sloping Hillside

desc:
There seems to be no end to these plains. While looking over yet another grassy
ridge, all one can see is sloping ground that spreads out towards the horizon.
Travelling to the north seems easier than to the other directions, since going
that way is mostly downhill. Luckily, there are several animal paths to help a
traveller maintain his direction on the way through this featureless landscape.
name: Undulating Plains

desc:
The grass grows high and thick here, covering most of the land. In the west the
sparkle of the Anduin river reflects in the fields, the furious sound of its
rushing water echoes even this far away. Bare scraped branches of small bushes
and trampled flowers witness a great deal of activity here. Food is plentiful
for the animals, and water can be found only a couple of miles to the west in
the clear current of the Anduin.
name: Fields of Green

desc:
The ground on the bottom of this depression is muddy and it looks like it may be
sinking down. Roots and loosely hanging branches of the tall bushes growing here
hinder movement in most places, while the plains surrounding the hollow seem to
offer easier paths for travellers. A puddle of water in the middle of this
depression is full of rotting leaves and moss; the field is slowly turning into
a peat bog.
name: A Bushy Hollow

desc:
These sparsely wooded ridges offers good viewpoints over the plains for anyone
willing to climb up the ridge here. A mounted traveller, however, would have no
choice but to ride through the fields below the ridges, where the terrain seems
perfect for setting up ambushes or lookouts. Decent paths are nowhere to be
found, yet the old remains of a campsite among some rocks suggest that someone
passes through here occasionally.
name: Ridged Plains

desc:
A wooded ridge, shaped like a crescent moon, separates this dry field from the
bordering plains. Dense brushwood surrounds the field completely, leaving only
one opening at the side of the ridge where passing through is easier. A huge log
has been dragged to the middle of the field where it serves as a bench; the
circle of sooty stones lying in front of the log seems to have held campfires in
the past. At the inner side of the ridge, an entrance to a small cave lies
almost hidden by a pair of bushes.
name: A Campsite amidst a Brushwood

desc:
Wind, rain and a lack of vegetation have turned this part of the plains into a
bare field of soil and gravel. Hardy weeds and twigs cling to sheltered spots
near some larger rocks, but other than that the landscape is completely barren.
A dry streambed leads to the west, towards areas where bushes thrive among
richer soil.
name: A Gravelly Field

desc:
The gravelly remains of a trail leads westwards, past meadows and small stands
of trees, towards a field of shrubs. In the east, the trail becomes too
overgrown to follow, and it ends near a lonely elm that stands upon a hillock. A
narrow ditch following the trail bends to the north, where it too gets engulfed
by dense vegetation under a gentle slope.
name: Remnants of a Trail

desc:
Large rocks covered by mosses and lichen stand in this field of dry hay. The
ground slopes up towards a stand of young trees in the west and down towards
grassy fields in other directions. The high hay conceals some smaller rocks,
which might make movement without stumbling over them hard, but would also
provide cover for small animals scurrying from one hiding place to another.
name: A Rocky Slope

desc:
Two sooty walls built of logs are all that remains standing of this destroyed
farmhouse. There seems to have been a fire; the bark on the trees near the house
is somewhat charred and piles of blackened debris lie near the walls. The house
also had a fireplace and a chimney, but of those only a pile of rubble remains.
Stinging nettles and other tall plants thrive in the rich soil among the ruins.
name: A Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
The gravelly remains of a road can be seen under the vegetation here. An old
milestone still stands on a visible spot in front of a shrubbery; it seems that
the main road used to turn to the south while a smaller road, wide enough for a
cart, branched off to the west. However, no signs of heavy traffic are visible,
and the short bushes growing on the lane suggest that there has not been any in
years.
name: An Old Crossing

desc:
A solid, wooden fence surrounds the trampled field of grass here; this is
clearly a pasture, since there are also signs of grazing and soft heaps of dung
on the ground. The pasture is connected to a paddock in the east, with only a
rickety gate standing in between, and the roof of a farmhouse is visible farther
away. A compost pile lies behind the fence near the gate; the weeds thriving in
the rich soil fail to cover up the strong smell of decay.
name: A Pasture near a Farmhouse

desc:
No vegetation grows on this field of bare soil; the only signs of grass and
bushes are the sooty sticks and blackened leaves scattered on the ground. Heaps
of stumps and large rocks stand here and there in the open ground, and it looks
like this area is being turned into a cultivated field. The clusters of hay on a
small hillock in the south have also been burnt away; recent rains have made the
loose soil slide down the slope, exposing the bedrock at the hillside.
name: Bare Soil

desc:
Waist-high rocks, covered in lichen, and young trees growing in thick clusters
hinder movement on the bottom of this small valley situated between the slopes
in the north and in the south. The grassy plains extending to the west gradually
turn into heathlands here, and the hardy weeds are replaced by evergreen sprigs
bearing berries of many different colours. In the east, the vale ends in a
sparsely wooded heath.
name: Heathlands at the Edge of Fields

desc:
Tussocks of yellowed grass and weeds cover the bumps and depressions in these
treeless fields. The ground is dry and sandy, but it appears even more so in the
east where the fields slowly turn into a sparsely wooded heath. In some places,
a bare field of sand with no vegetation other than the occasional tufts of grass
spreads out. Any tracks would be clearly visible there, if the wind did not wipe
them out regularly.
name: Bumpy, Dry Fields

desc:
An outcrop of rock, shaped like a giant clenched fist, breaks the monotony of
the fields spreading out everywhere around it. The ground slopes steeply upwards
in the east, where a clear path leads around the ledge to the fields above.
Boulders about the size of a pony lie scattered around the outcrop, while
smaller ones dot the fields in the west.
name: Under a Rocky Ledge

desc:
The bedrock is quite close to the surface here, since masses of bare rock stick
out of the dry fields of hay in some places. In the west, a rocky ledge breaks
the grassy slope leading to the plains below, but there seems to be a path going
around it. The few trees growing above the ledge are stunted; a small wood of
birches stands much farther away, thriving in the rich soil of the moors that
extend to the east.
name: Dry Fields above a Ledge

desc:
The high, yellowed hay growing in these windswept fields rustles quietly even
when the air stands still. Cracks run through the dry mud in places where the
water formed puddles during last rains, and the ground is hard like rock in most
places. Small piles of fist-sized stones can be seen among the hay near the
rockier grounds in the east; grooves in the sand amidst the stones hint at the
presence of small rodents, lizards or even snakes.
name: Dry Fields of Hay

desc:
A field of high hay, clear of any bushes or small trees that would make reaping
difficult, spreads out here. This is the type of hay that farmers grow for
feeding the cattle and keeping their floors warm in the winter; there are even
some stacks left rotting around the sharp wooden poles standing in the fields. A
small path enters the hay in the north, and it seems to be leading towards yet
another cultivated field in the west.
name: Fields of Hay

desc:
Tall thistles with thorny leaves and stems grow among the grass in these
undulating fields. There are no clear paths through the vegetation, and in some
places the tangled grass and plants have grown tall enough to stick to boots and
legs, making movement hard. Some boulders stand in the fields, half hidden by
grass and thistles, and much more than just rocks might lie unseen in the
undergrowth. While standing, however, one can be seen from afar, since there are
hardly any trees or large bushes growing in these fields.
name: Thistles in the Grass

desc:
Muddy furrows surrounded by wild weeds lead the water away from these fields
towards the streambed in the northwest. It seems like someone tried to grow
wheat here, but either there was too much rain or the ground was simply too wet,
since there are blackened and mouldy ears of wheat bending to the ground. Some
cart tracks lead past the field in the northeast, while a small dirt path
branches off that trail and leads to the south through the field. Gentle slopes
separate this wet field from the higher grounds in the southeast.
name: Furrowed Fields

desc:
A trickling stream has carved a muddy furrow deep into the grassy ground here.
Embankments of loose soil rise steeply above the streambed on both sides, making
climbing up to the fields in the north or in the south difficult. The water of
the stream is surprisingly clear regardless of the surroundings; tiny insects
can be seen swimming in the water and crawling over the brown leaves that cover
the bottom.
name: A Narrow Stream through the Grasslands

desc:
This is a small field that spreads out in front of a cabin. It seems to be used
for growing potatoes, carrots and probably other vegetables as well. The tops
ends of the vegetables stick out of the soil in neat rows, with shallow furrows
keeping them separate. A pile of ash, burnt leaves and sooty pieces of wood lies
behind the patch. No flames are rising from the pile, but the steaming ground
indicates that something was burnt here recently. A footpath leads from the
fields to the cabin in the south.
name: A Potato Patch

desc:
Here, a peaceful meadow of flowers spreads out under a huge willow. A pile of
large, round stones stands under the tree; the stones are sandy and brown, as if
they were dug out of the ground only recently. There are some cart tracks on the
ground, and it looks like the stones were brought here instead of having been
gathered in the immediate surroundings. A small path leads to the north, where a
thick growth of stinging nettles separates this meadow from what seems to be a
small cabin.
name: A Small Cairn

desc:
Some hollow trunks are all that remains of a small patch of forest that once
grew here. There must be at least a dozen of these lying around on this meadow
alone, and the high hay in the south might conceal more. The trunks are very dry
and scarred, and there are small splinters and pieces of fungus on the ground.
It looks like someone has gathered a supply of touchwood here, though it could
be that these markings are made by just animals. Some living trees still stand
far away to the east.
name: Fallen Trunks

desc:
Thick mud and watery depressions makes moving on the bottom of the ditch
difficult. A small stream runs towards the Anduin here, but following it is hard
since it occasionally fades into the mud and vegetation. Grassy fields extend
north and south, but there does not seem to be an easy way to get over or out of
this wide, muddy ditch.
name: A Muddy Ditch

desc:
The murky waters of a oblong-shaped pond fill the wide ditch that runs through
the plains here. The pool of water is surrounded by a field of sinking, deep
mud, which makes walking there really hard and riding impossible. Long rushes
stick out of the mud, their brown heads brushing against anything trying to make
its way past the pond. Farther away from the pond, a steep embankment rises
above the mud at the edge of some grassy fields.
name: A Pool Surrounded by Rushes

desc:
A narrow, gravelly path gently curves to the west as it passes through a group
of short, thorny bushes. It seems like these are the remains of a road that once
led from the settlements in the northeast to the south and towards the river.
Vegetation now covers this level strip of land, but the ground is hard and there
are some rusty pieces of metal half buried into the gravel, hinting at some
traffic in the past. Cracked, dead branches hang off the sides of some bushes;
it seems like they used to block the path earlier.
name: A Vanishing Path

desc:
Two dead oaks stand tall in the middle of this field, surrounded by patches of
flowers. Only one crooked branch remains on the taller tree; the rest seem to
have been cracked off by strong winds and fallen into a dry tangle on the
ground. The shorter of the two trees died only recently, and caps from its last
acorns still cling to the thin branches. Grassy fields spread out in all
directions around the pair of trees.
name: A Pair of Dead Trees

desc:
Ancient stumps of what were once mighty trees stand out among the mossy rocks in
this bumpy field of hay. The thick branches that are lying on the ground are
grey and without bark on them, and it seems like the trees that grew here were
cut down many years ago. Still, there are some recent axe cuts on one stump, and
splinters lying on the ground around it; someone has been here looking for
firewood.
name: A Field of Stumps and Branches

desc:
Clusters of wild wheat grow in this smooth field. Two poles that might once have
held the hinges of a gate stand at the edge of the field - an edge where one can
also see the end of a trail. The trail leads to the east past similar fields and
overgrown pastures. No signs of cattle are visible, but some droppings on the
ground hint at game animals coming here to graze.
name: An Old Pasture

desc:
Smooth grass covers the ancient ruins of a village here. There seems to have
been at least two dozen houses built of logs, but most of their woodwork has
rotted away or slowly sunk into the ground over the years. The structures that
were built of stone stand out better; the foundation of a large hall lies in the
middle of an open field, surrounded by the other ruins. While looking into a few
spots behind the large grassy mounds that used to be houses, a collapsed
underground storage and a large stone well can be noticed among the rubble.
name: Ruins of a Village

desc:
Among the trees growing in these dry fields, someone has piled up rocks about
the size of a man's head into mounds several yards wide. Perhaps the ground was
being cleared for planting a field; perhaps the mounds were built for burial. In
any case, whatever took place here happened many years ago; some of the smaller
mounds are now almost entirely covered in grass.
name: Rocky Mounds in the Fields

desc:
The fields here are dotted by knee-high stones covered in velvety evergreen
moss. Tiny ferns clinging to the sides of the stones seem to be the only plants
thriving in these dry meadows; the grass is yellowed and all the flowers seem to
have withered away before growing to full height. A group of rowans standing on
a hillock seems to be the best landmark on the otherwise level landscape.
name: Among the Stones

desc:
Thick boards have been laid down to cover up the dirt floor of this small cabin,
but there is still bare ground near the door and around the crude stone oven
that sits in one of the corners. There is no fire in the oven right now, but the
stones are quite warm and are radiating heat into the whole building. A wooden
table and a pair of benches, carved out of huge logs, stands near the back wall.
A piece of bark attached to a metal holder is burning above the table, giving
faint light to the room. There is a rack close to the ceiling, possibly meant to
store food or supplies.
name: A Farmer's Hut

desc:
This paddock is surrounded by a sturdy wooden fence with a gate leading out to
the pastures in the west. The fence connects with the back wall of a farmhouse
in the east, where a small door leads inside. A heap of hay has been left on a
rack at one corner of the fence for the animals to eat, and there are also some
cereals from a better meal remaining on the ground. Some water is dripping to
the ground from a trough standing next to the rack.
name: An Animal Paddock

desc:
A long table, with benches lying on both sides, stands in the middle of the main
hall of this farmhouse. A wall made of huge logs separates the hall from a
cramped kitchen, while the chimney leading down through the wall turns into a
fireplace on this side and into an oven inside the kitchen. Some beds lie in the
far corner of the hall, surrounded by a curtain that can be pulled around them
for some privacy. There also seems to be a large bedroom at the end of a short
hallway near the main entrance to the house.
name: Inside a Large Farmhouse

desc:
The water on the basement floor is less than two feet deep, and a barely
noticeable current suggests that it is somehow connected to the river. The piles
of debris in the water consist of floorboards, broken cogwheels and other parts
of the old woodwork. Most of it is buried in the mud; the wood has not rotted
away over the years, but recovering any parts would be hard work. Two wooden
beams lean against the wall, making climbing in and out of the basement a bit
easier.
name: A Watery Basement

desc:
This quiet pond, surrounded by a thick shrubbery, lies here on the bottom of a
hollow in the plains. The pond is somewhat overgrown; thick growths of moss,
hardly solid enough to walk on, cover its surface near the banks, and dense
bushes makes it hard to reach the water in most places. The hollow continues to
the south, where one can spot some trees among the shrubs.
name: A Shallow Pond

desc:
The ground here seems to be too dry for most weeds and flowers, but hardy shrubs
cover the patches of sand and gravel where even the widespread carpet of grass
has died away. It seems like there have been better seasons for growth, since
many of the bushes are still quite massive. In many places they have gathered
into dense shrubberies, where small animals can seek cover from the birds of
prey that can sometimes be seen circling in the sky far above.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Some rust-coloured leaves still cling to the branches of the bushes growing
here, but much more can be found drying up on the ground. The bushes rustle
quietly in the breeze as their branches scrape against each other, with leaves
and sticks making a crackling noise underneath. Still, it seems possible to move
among the bushes undetected; even without leaves they are concealing, and it is
hard to tell if any noises are caused by anything but the wind.
name: Dry Shrubs

desc:
Moving on these sloping fields can be somewhat straining at times, but the wide
plains in the north and in the west seem to offer easier ways for travelling. A
steep slope, covered in short hay and clusters of heather, leads up to the
southeast, where the short bushes growing here are replaced by tall pine trees.
Rain water has carved sandy furrows into the ground; they lead down the slope
through the vegetation.
name: Rising Plains

desc:
This slope separates the heathlands in the southeast from the plains below.
Climbing along the slope is hard; the paths leading past large boulders and
moss-covered outcrops of rock are impassable in many places, while loose peat
and moisture make the ground slippery. Slender rowans grow in the few places
where trees can easily take root; most of the slope remains barren, even though
water is trickling through the cracks in the cliff.
name: A Rugged Slope

desc:
Following the sharp turn west the river just made it now eases back onto the
original path south. The roaring madness of the river further north is calming
down here. Only occasional white swirls can be seen as the water hits a
concealed rock or tree trunk. To the west, the elsewhere so scattered trees form
a haven for themselves, growing closer and closer together. Beyond this weald
the marshy lands of Gladden Fields can be spotted under the mist.
name: Anduin Swirls

desc:
The Anduin stream takes a rather sharp turn to the west here. Gazing further
south the river quickly moves back to its original path though, heading south
through the green landscape. Anything entering the water would quickly be
dragged into the rushing waves in the centre of the flood, and then swept away.
name: Turn in the Anduin

desc:
In the centre of this stream water rages like it wanted to crush the white foam
on its own waves. Rocks and stones peek up from the sandy bottom, but never
break the surface. Their presence is still enough to upset the river. Small
trees grow close to the shore flourishing in the presence of the clear water.
name: Rapids of the Anduin

desc:
The river runs past a rocky outcrop in the east, slowly eating away the cliff.
Farther away in the south it meets a steep, grassy embankment and bends slightly
to the west. The bottom of the river is not visible here, but it should not be
too deep since some rocks are sticking out of the water near the cliffs.
Branches and dead trunks floating in the river have gathered into a tangle among
some of the largest ones.
name: The Anduin Passing a Steep Cliff

desc:
Here, the river meets a low cliff in the south, then turns gently to the west
where it continues on its course through the plains. Some of the trees growing
on the eastern bank gather the litter floating along the surface into their
hanging branches while leaning over the river. Their roots also reach into the
water, forming hiding places for fishes and water birds.
name: A Bend in the Anduin

desc:
Sharp rocks and surges on the water make the river look quite challenging here.
However, going downstream should be easy for an experienced boater or swimmer.
The river bends westwards, where the water loses some of its speed as the
riverbed gets wider. Near the eastern bank, a muddy stream joining the Anduin
makes the water slightly clouded.
name: Swiftly Flowing Waters of the Anduin

desc:
The river flows in a straight southward direction for about a mile here. The
current seems strong and the landscape is constantly changing. The banks appear
to slope downwards in the south, but the difference is hardly noticeable on the
water. The river is quite deep in most places, yet some reeds and rocks can be
seen above the surface downstream of here.
name: The Anduin River

desc:
The river flows gently here, passing wet fields and the occasional shrubbery or
stand of trees. To the south, the river bends westwards where it meets a beach;
a beach that actually seems to be an exposed part of the river's sandy bottom.
The field of sand continues far away from the waters edge, so a part of it
should stay above the surface even during floods.
name: A Bend in the Anduin

desc:
The course of the river suddenly turns to the south, where the water seems to
gain more speed. The river also gets quite deep here, and one cannot see the
bottom further than a few yards away from the bank. Swift currents make ripples
on the surface, and fishes splash in places where they nibble at insects and
litter moving along the water.
name: The Great River Anduin

desc:
Several rocky islets rise a few feet above the water near the northern bank of
the river. Some have grass and stunted trees growing on them, some smaller ones
are only covered by bird droppings, although it seems like they might also be
used for nesting. The river bends southwards as it passes the bushy banks in the
west.
name: Small Islets in the River Anduin

desc:
A long, grassy island near the western bank splits the river into two separate
streams. The upstream part of the island is overgrown with reeds, wild lilies
and other plants thriving in the shallow water, whereas only clusters of rushes
can be seen growing in the wider of the two branches. In the southeast, some
cliffs stand above the river, and the currents seem strong in the murky waters
below.
name: The Great River Anduin

desc:
Clear water rushes furiously southwards, dragging with it everything that dares
to cross its path. Closer to the shore it calms down, but it still moves with
distinct determination. A low slope separates the water from the grassy fields
beyond; on these sides animals visit frequently to drink from the water.
name: Rushing Anduin

desc:
Bending back to its original path to the south the river stretches out, cutting
through the green landscape. Behind a couple of rocks peeking up from the bottom
of the river, shadows of resting fish reflect in the clear but disturbed
surface. The water flows quickly southwards carving its way down in the soft
earth, leaving the banks numerous feet high.
name: Southward Stretch of the Anduin

desc:
This mud-covered slope quickly drops towards the west into what appears to be a
mud pit. As the earth slides down the slope underfoot, it threatens to take
anything standing on this hill with it. No trees or large plants grow here to
offer handhold and no grass that would offer foothold could survive in this
highly moist environment. Moving up the northern slope seems the easiest way out
of this muddy mess.
name: Muddy Slope

desc:
Water high enough to soak even the highest boot covers the land, turning this
basin into a swamp. Water reeds and lilies grow in abundance. Wading through is
possible here but becomes more difficult in the north. The reeds eventually
become so thick, they totally block all sight of the land northward. High levees
have been constructed to the east and south of here to reign in the water and
protect the roads and settlements just beyond.
name: Field of Water Reeds

desc:
Suddenly, the mud beneath you begins to sink, taking you down with it. Reeds and
water plants tangle up your legs and arms, refusing to allow departure. In every
direction, mud contains you, weighing down your limbs, making it extremely
difficult to move quickly enough to free yourself from the reeds. And slowly,
you sink further into the mud.
name: Mud Trap

desc:
The earth has become saturated here by an unseen water source. Grass still grows
but dies quickly as the earth to the south and west rapidly turns to mud. To the
north and east, hills rise out of this basin. To the south, the earth continues
to slope downward very quickly. Thick water reeds obscure vision in that
direction. Travel in that direction would be difficult indeed.
name: Muddy Grasslands

desc:
A muddy mix of brush, water plants and earth engulf any creature who travels
here. Movement becomes tortuous as arms and legs are caked with heavy mud. The
stagnant water sits here almost as deep as a hobbit is tall, but it is so thick
with mud that wading through it is like trying to move through warm wax. The
easiest way out of this tiring mess seems to be back north. A blooming
water-lily floats on the surface of the water.
name: In the Mire

desc:
A thick, gooey, muddy mess slows your descent into this basin. The stagnant
water and the earth have merged into a thick, unyielding mud that threatens to
swallow adventurous travellers whole. Strong water reeds, almost floating in the
muddy earth, offer even more resistance to any who attempt to move through this
area. They snag on clothes, in hair, or anything else and hang on so tightly it
almost seems that they have a sense of purpose. The earth seems to slope
downwards even more directly to the east.
name: Depression in the Mud Plains

desc:
Knee-high to a man, water floods these lands and is contained by the levees and
hills to the south and east. Water lilies and reeds thrive in this watery basin,
while the grass and earth under the water have become a muddy and difficult
surface to walk upon. To the south, a mud-covered hill leads up to the Old
Forest Road but is impassable from this depression, as the slope is moderately
steep and too slick to find foothold.
name: Field of Water Reeds

desc:
All around are various water grasses and reeds, some as tall as a pony. The
earth is so water-soaked in this area that a man would sink in to his knees
standing here. Walking through this area is difficult enough but riding proves
to be impossible. The earth seems a bit more solid to the north and east of here
but venturing farther south could be more difficult.
name: Among the Water Plants

desc:
It's difficult to tell whether the substance surrounding your feet is muddy
water or watery mud. Movement through this area is less like walking and more
like wading. Whatever water source supplies this depression must be very near.
Rising above the water-covered land to the south, the Old Forest Road can be
seen running east and west.
name: In the Mire

desc:
An underground water source exits the earth in this depression, flooding the
land for miles around. The water immediately mixes with the brown earth to form
watery, messy paste that sticks to everything. To the west and south, steep
muddy slopes keep the water contained and off the Old Forest Road. Coming down
those slopes seems easy enough but travelling back up through the mud might not
be possible.
name: At a Large Spring

desc:
At a meeting of the Old Forest Road and a wide footpath, a large, well-guarded
Beorning settlement has prospered. Completely encased in a wall of trees and
brush, the village gates open to the Old Forest Road. The road leaves the
village heading west and bends northwards as it cuts a swath through the vale.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Although the road bends and runs to the north and east, the footfalls of many
men and animals have worn the edges of the road away. What was a sharp bend in
the Old Forest Road is now a gentle curve. In the east, the road leads to a
small Beorning village. In the north, the road continues for a short time before
turning back to the west.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
This patch of barren ground near a bend in the Old Forest Road has been trampled
so often by travellers it appears to almost be part of the road. Those following
the road from the north often cut through here shortening their journey a few
miles before re-entering the road east of here. Often these travellers are in a
hurry to reach the small settlement that causes an abrupt end to the eastward
path of the Old Forest Road.
name: Worn Earth

desc:
Insects buzz about, oblivious to anyone or anything but their work. This close
to the Old Forest Road, the grass that covers much of this land is noticeably
absent. It apparently could not survive the heavy off-road foot traffic. In its
place, weeds, as oblivious to travellers as the insects are, grow despite the
footfalls of man and beast that crushed the life out of the grass.
name: Wild Weeds

desc:
Bees and other insects hover around, gathering nectar from the variety of
wildflowers that grow here. In opposition of the plant life that makes up a vast
portion of the Anduin Vales, no grass grows in this area near the road. The only
plants that can survive this close to a well-travelled road such as the Old
Forest Road are weeds, and they grow here is abundance.
name: Wild Weeds

desc:
This field is covered with wide expanses of weeds and flowers in the northern
part and thick grass and low bushes in the southern part. Presence of living
organisms shows itself by bees buzzing around in the weeds, and ants and beetles
crawling around in the grass. The surrounding fields to the north and west are
also covered with weeds, while to the south and east the flat plains are covered
with thick high grasses and an occasional bush.
name: A Field Covered with Weeds and Grass

desc:
This field is covered in thick tufts of weeds with slender, sharp-edged leaves.
Tiny, vine-like plants are creeping along the branches of the few young trees
and berry bushes growing here. Also noticeable, are some patches of wild
strawberries among the weeds. The grasslands seem to continue in all directions;
a slope leads down to a small pond in the south, while in the north a small hill
rising above the plains might offer a better view over the landscape.
name: A Field of Weeds

desc:
The open plains are covered with various grasses, some high and some low. Other
annual vegetation raise slightly above the grass, thereby attracting insects to
its sparse flowers. To the north the vegetation gets lower and instead of grass
mostly weeds seem to cover the soil.
name: Open Plains

desc:
The grasses here seem to be more thick and dense as compared to those to the
west. Various flowers and other plants, standing out from the grass, can be seen
scattered around the fields. Faint tracks lead to a small hole nearly hidden
below the grass. Similar fields stretch on in all directions, though the ground
appears to turn rockier and less fertile to the south.
name: Dense Plains

desc:
The northern part of these fields, which lie just south of a well-travelled
road, are covered with weeds. Contrary to this, the uneven southern part is
covered with dense grasses and an occasional sapling of cottonwood or willow. To
the far south small fences surrounding cultivated fields can be spotted.
name: Uneven Fields

desc:
The largest part of this landscape is covered with grass and small saplings of
trees. However, the bare soil, imprinted with tracks, is visible through the
sparse weeds in the northeastern corner of these fields, as if travellers
following the road cut a corner to shorten their journey. Far to the south, some
farm buildings are barely visible above the horizon.
name: Fields bordering the Old Forest Road

desc:
A quiet pond lies here in the middle of an open field of grass. The sandy bottom
of the pond is visible a few feet away from the bank, but then the water seems
to suddenly get much deeper; no rushes or water lilies can be seen growing
farther away from the shore. The carpet of grass is wet and sinking near the
bank and it would be easy to get one's boots soaked there. A dry path leads away
from the pond, starting at the southern bank, where someone has built a small
jetty.
name: A Small Pond

desc:
This field is covered with varied species of grass. Some tussocks, with long
stalks with white stripes running from top to bottom, are standing out between
the otherwise dark green grasses. A partly burned tree, apparently struck by
lightning, provides a viewing point for birds that prey on the small animals
living in the grasslands.
name: Wild Fields

desc:
Some patches of wild wheat are fighting for space among the bushes and young
trees in these overgrown fields. It seems like there was once a wide field of
corn here, but now only one small patch in the south seems well-tended. A pile
of boards that looks like the remains of a small barn lies hidden amidst a
thorny shrubbery in the middle of the field. A narrow dirt path starting from
the door of a farmhouse in the west leads down a grassy slope towards a pond far
in the north.
name: Overgrown Fields

desc:
Two tall elms shade this smooth meadow with their reaching branches. The one
with the thicker trunk is forked near the ground, making it easy to climb; there
are also some markings on its bark. A thick rope, firmly attached from both ends
to a branch several feet above the ground, sways gently back and forth. A circle
of flowering shrubs surrounds the meadow completely; the sweet fragrance that is
barely noticeable on their dry leaves must fill the air on warm summer nights.
name: A Peaceful Meadow under the Elms

desc:
A smooth layer of moss covers the soil and the large rocks sticking out of the
ground here. The vegetation gets dense towards a shady depression in the south,
while a field of hay extends to the west. No trees grow in this rocky area; only
thick ferns and the woody stems of short perennial shrubs rise above the
evergreen carpet of moss.
name: Rocky Ground Bordering the Moors

desc:
The ground here is wet and the thick weeds that did not make it through the last
winter are slowly rotting away, turning into yet another layer of peat. It seems
like there once was a pond here; a cluster of rushes is withering on the bottom
of a depression, where the carpet of moss makes a squelching sound as it sinks
under anything heavier than a rabbit. Shrubs with leathery, oblong leaves are
thriving in the fields surrounding the depression.
name: Low Moors

desc:
The lonely birches and pines here are surrounded by a widespread growth of
heather. On the ground, the tiny pink flowers fallen off the heathers have
rolled into small piles and turned mostly brown. The dry needles and sticks
rustle quietly around anything moving through these heathlands, and the knocking
sounds of a woodpecker can be heard at times somewhere in the distance.
name: Patches of Heather

desc:
The sandy ground is tightly packed up by the roots of various bushes and plants
with woody stems growing here. The slope is steepest down in the north, where
the heath seems to continue as far as the eye can see, but there are also some
dangerous-looking spots on the rocky slope in the west. Stumps, mounds and other
odd shapes lie amidst the tangled undergrowth; clear paths are scarce here.
name: Sloping Heathlands

desc:
Harsh ground to the west slowly gives way to a more hospitable environment.
Scattered shrubs grow in sporadic clusters, while traces of heather intermingle
with other wild flowers. The ground itself is a mixture of sand and rich farming
soil. Aside from a make-shift trail that serves no apparent purpose, the land
seems mostly unaltered from its natural state. A faint path leads from a ruined
building in the east to a cottage in the north.
name: Edge of a Heath

desc:
At the centre of these lightly wooded surroundings stands a small cottage, which
appears to have been abandoned some time ago. It is made completely of rough
stone except for the primitive wooden door which barely sticks out through the
thick overgrowth. Jutting up from the roof, a small chimney points to the sky.
Five long, flat rocks create steps leading up to the humble abode.
name: Abandoned Cottage

desc:
The rough stones forming the walls of this cottage are covered with thick layers
of moss. The front door, still sturdy despite the very damp air and deep dents
carved by sharp blades, provides an effective closure to the outside world.
Remains of furniture and household items are scattered on the floor, while in
the far corner various bones, some probably humanoid of origin, lie partly
covered between some ragged clothing.
name: Inside a Cottage

desc:
The ground is soft and squishy underfoot. Creeping fig has taken over the
majority of this soft landscape, creating a beautiful dark green blanket of
small round leaves. A few young cottonwoods struggle to break free from the
foliage. To the south an old cottage can be spotted.
name: Blanketed Meadows

desc:
This field is covered with wide expanses of grass and flowers without any
obstructions for large animals or humanoids. Hoofprints of those animals
visiting these abundant fields during wet days have been captured in the muddy
soil. On the lower somewhat sheltered grounds leaves litter the ground, dwarfing
tiny ants that carry them.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The fields are covered with grass, weeds, and low bushes. A large willow has
toppled over, creating a long barrier of thick boughs and thin branches to grow
out from its top side. In the eastern part of the fields, a half-erect pile of
wood, most likely a poorly constructed shelter barely able to stand, sits in the
fields. To the west a pair of elms form a natural landmark.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Numerous little holes fill the serene grasslands, it is most likely an
underground tunnelling system of some indigenous rodents. The grasslands drop
down steeply in the east, ending in a rocky slope leading to a crevice. The
divide is shallow at first, allowing travelling down into it, but is already
twice the height of a Beorning further to the east.
name: Grasslands near a Crevice

desc:
Leafless trees, signs of a flourishing past, mark these dry fields. Between the
dusty soil small tussocks of grass and patches of low weed fight for the little
water which is left underground. Apparently the ground water flow has been
altered radically by the tectonic movement in the past, from which the result, a
crevice stretching many miles south, is visible intersecting the fields.
name: Dusty Fields

desc:
The ground here is rocky and hard. Running to the east for miles lies a long
strip of ruptured earth. It appears the ground gave way to the emerging stone
after some sort of tectonic shift under the surface. Though shallow and narrow
enough to pose no obstruction to man nor beast here, it seems to have acted as a
natural barrier to the local flora, separating different types of naturally
growing plants and wild flowers. To the south the ground is split apart by a
deep crevice.
name: Ruptured Fields

desc:
Fields from the north and south meet here at what appears to be a natural stone
barrier, running for miles to the east and west. The stone is short and easily
walked over, but it seems to have acted as a hindrance to the low-lying vines on
its west side. It is likely the protruding stone originated from some
subterranean shift or movement.
name: Ruptured Fields

desc:
A short, natural barricade of stone protrudes from the ground, rupturing the
fields in an unbroken line. While it stretches for miles to the west, it seems
to taper off slowly in height steadily towards the northeast. Some small patches
of dead grass lie on top of the six feet wide protrusion. A few pine trees are
scattered about, one of which apparently was toppled by the rock emerging from
underneath its base.
name: Ruptured Fields

desc:
A lush, verdant landscape is offset only by the slight smell of fresh manure.
Short-cropped patches of grass dotted with daisies cover the nearby soil, while
from some distance way the sound of mooing comes from the silhouettes of cattle.
A single oak tree towers above and provides an almost unnaturally large canopy.
In the distance to the north, a hand-crafted rock wall no more than four feet
tall intersects the fields.
name: Grazing Fields

desc:
The surroundings depict a pasture full of local animal life. The grass grows
tall and seems healthy. A hand-crafted rock wall runs down from the north of
this place of solace to a crevice in the southwest where it is joined by a
wooden fence, which keeps the herds at a safe distance from the steep edge.
name: Secluded Pasture

desc:
At the centre of this field lies a giant depression, full of natural waste. It
seems its caretakers are meticulous about its planning and upkeep, because no
detritus is on the ground around it. It is impossible to tell how deep the pit
goes, but its contents fill it to the point of staying flush with the ground.
Surrounding the pit, grass and other vegetation seem especially healthy.
name: Compost Pit

desc:
Starting from the farmstead which stands in the east, these fields show a
variety of crops; carrots, potatoes and cabbage are all growing here. Among the
fields you can see one that lies fallow revealing the rich, black soil
characteristic of the farms found in this area. A wooden fence separates the
most western fields from the edge of a ravine.
name: Vegetable Fields

desc:
The cultivated rye fields are separated by low earthen walls. The rye grows over
a foot high, with the occasional blue flower popping up here and there. Some
small tracks lead through the dense fields to a partly overgrown trail in the
east. A wooden fence prevents careless animals or others from walking over the
edge of the fields onto a pile of loose rocks leading into a deep crevice.
name: Rye Fields

desc:
The soft ground is covered by green plants and tall grasses. A row of huge logs
covered with mosses separates the fields from the path in the south. Dark
bushes, covered with small black berries grow at the eastern edge of the fields,
while some of the plants in the middle of the field seem to hide some sort of
vegetable under it. To the east the fields come to an abrupt ending at a very
deep and quite wide crevice.
name: Untamed Fields

desc:
The surrounding area seems to be quite flat, allowing a broad view. A beaten
path ending at a steep slope leading down is surrounded by small plants and grey
stones. There are a few bushes to the north and south,impeding travel somewhat
in these directions. To the west three ropes forming a very simple bridge span a
deep crevice.
name: Trail near a Deep Crevice

desc:
A small ruin constructed of rough grey stones stands between the fig and vines.
The walls are overgrown with ivy and other plants, suggesting that it has been
in disrepair for a long long time already. Some old rotten planks fill an
opening which once used to be the entrance. Behind these boards, dense bushes
fill the the whole interior. Around the shack, rough fields continue in all
directions.
name: Overgrown Ruin

desc:
The meadow here has been completely enveloped in a rich, green blanket of
low-lying fig. The vines seem healthy and vast, stretching for miles to the
north and south. Moisture seems to stay trapped under the dense leaves, keeping
the ground below soft and cool; an ideal environment for bugs and spiders. An
old set of wooden fence posts have almost succumbed to the vines, with just
their tips appearing above the thick foliage.
name: Blanketed Meadows

desc:
The ground here seems especially soft, but it is unclear if that is due to the
thick foliage or soft ground below the fallen leaves. The slightly rolling hills
are almost completely consumed by a low-lying creeping fig. A slight stench
rises from the ground, indicating the recent death of something small hidden
beneath the leaves. Close to the west stands a petrified tree, grasping at the
sky with its claw-like branches.
name: Blanketed Meadows

desc:
The surrounding plots of lands appear to have been lush and green in the past,
but now only bald stems and trunks, filled with small holes from termites, are
left from that era. The former spring is covered with a hard crust of mud in
which here and there a skeleton of a fish or small animal has been trapped. The
mud spreads a malodorous smell over the surrounding plains.
name: Dried Out Spring

desc:
The earth here is covered with various knee-deep grasses and an occasional broom
bush with slender green stems and very small leaves. From the dead leaves
beneath these bushes the noise of small animals, like hedgehogs or mice, nosing
around for worms can be heard. To the northwest the plains raise slightly before
coming to an abrupt end at a deep ravine, while to the south the plains dip
slightly.
name: Plains at the Edge of a Ravine

desc:
The path, leading to an old hut in the west, is marked with granite stones,
which are mostly hidden under huge piles of leaves. The raised northern and
