southern edges of the path shelter it from strong winds. To the east it ends at
the edge of a crevice. However, a flimsy bridge constructed out of three ropes
spans the crevice, enabling travel to the other side.
name: End of an Overgrown Trail

desc:
Broken-up farmland stretches in all directions. The dark and nutrient-rich soil
shows itself through the occasional break in the rye patches. Riding uniformly
along the western edge of the fields stand dozens of old fence posts. They may
once have prevented unwanted wildlife to this area, but now decomposition has
robbed them of their rails. To the east a deep crevice blocks passage to the
plains beyond it.
name: Abandoned Rye Fields

desc:
Stretching all around are abandoned fields with here and there a small patch of
rye. Though it looks as if it could have been productive land, all that remains
are a few rusty, corroded tools and an old steel plough half buried in the
ground. To the east, on the other side of the crevice, some cultivated fields
are visible.
name: Abandoned Farmland

desc:
The ground slants slightly to the northeast before ending at a deep ravine. The
once groomed fiels are now overgrown by wild weeds and other brush. A large part
of this vegetation has dried, which indicates the water that can be heard
dripping inside the ravine is not available for plants on the surface. At some
miles distance in the west a small open spot in the landscape can be spotted.
name: Slanted Fields

desc:
The bottom of the crevice consists of sharp loose rocks, making walking
difficult. An occasional tussock of grass grows in the dust between these rocks.
Almost vertical walls of solid rock rise north and south from the bottom of this
crevice. To the west the floor rises steadily towards the ground level of the
surrounding fields, to the east however, the floor lies many feet lower.
name: Shallow Crevice

desc:
Ferns, perfectly adapted to the shade, grow in small hollows in the claystone.
Between the sharp rocks small lizards crawl around looking for food. The
overhanging walls reflect the sounds of birds protecting their nests, which are
constructed on isolated ledges in the eastern wall.
name: Bend in a Dark Crevice

desc:
The crevice floor is covered with dark soil washed down from the steep slopes.
Various plants grow in the shade; bugleweed with green leaves with purple
highlights and purple flowers dot the middle part, while small ferns with light
green leaves cover the regions close to the overhanging walls.
name: A Deep, Dark Crevice

desc:
The crevice which splits the surrounding lands into two stretches from the
northwest to the southeast. The floor is covered with sharp pieces of quartzite
and other rocks. The northern wall has weathered unevenly, forming many small
ledges on which birds have built their nests.
name: Bend in a Dark Crevice

desc:
The bottom of the crevice is littered with small pieces of slate which are
covered with imprints of fern leaves. Streams of water drip from the eastern
rock face forming a small stream which disappears to the south beneath the loose
rocks.
name: A Dark, Narrow Crevice

desc:
The floor of the crevice is covered with loose rocks originating from the
eastern side. This eastern rock face has partly collapsed forming a huge rock
slide of sharp edged rocks which enables travelling in and out of the crevice by
a skilled climber. The occasional fern grows near the eastern wall which
consists of layers of slate on top of solid granite. The crevice stretches for
miles in the northward and southward directions.
name: A Rockslide in a Crevice

desc:
The crevice is bordered by overhanging rock walls to the west and east. The
bottom is covered with some huge boulders between which ferns and other shade
loving plants have found a habitat. A dried out hide of a snake is stuck on one
of the sharp edges of the boulders. To the south the crevice looks greener as if
water has found its way there.
name: Crevice

desc:
The bottom of the crevice is partly hidden beneath a pool of murky water with
reeds and ferns growing on its banks. Some beetles and salamanders and some
bubbles which emit a faint sulphurous smell disturb the surface. High above the
pool three ropes, forming a bridge, span the crevice from east to west.
name: Crevice beneath a Rope Bridge

desc:
The bridge, which is constructed out of three ropes, spans the crevice from west
to east allowing a somewhat safe route from one side of the crevice to the other
side. The thickest rope hangs several feet lower than the other two thinner
ones. Far below the ropes a murky pool covers the base of the crevice.
name: Rope Bridge

desc:
This small lodge had been erected by stacking thick wooden beams on top of each
other. A heavy wooden door marks its entrance, while a small window with
shutters gives a great view over the long crevice in the south. The interior of
this home consists of some simple furniture with a few paintings of farm animals
attached to the walls.
name: Herder's Lodge

desc:
The flow of water that is coming from the north brings dark, silt clouded water
into this portion of the Anduin River. Along the sides of the river plants seem
to struggle to survive despite the amount of water beside them. Brownish, dead
foliage of suffocated plants that were sucked into the river floats languidly
along on the surface.
name: The Anduin River
